topic: [en/idea_plan/robotics_control] Propose a novel control framework that combines model predictive control and reinforcement learning for agile quadrotor navigation through cluttered, dynamic en
full_prompt: Propose a novel control framework that combines model predictive control and reinforcement learning for agile quadrotor navigation through cluttered, dynamic environments.
completion:
Survey rationale: identify where MPC/RL quadrotor work stops—typically RL supplies tracking policies or learned costs, while MPC handles constraints, with limited treatment of dynamic clutter and uncertainty-aware interaction.  
```action search
query: "quadrotor agile navigation cluttered dynamic environments model predictive control reinforcement learning safe MPC learned policy"
discipline: robotics_control
top_k: 6
```
[result]: Candidate threads found: (i) deep drone racing / agile flight with end-to-end policies, DOI available but OpenAlex ID not resolved in this simulated pass; (ii) learning-based MPC / MPPI for quadrotors using learned dynamics or residual models; (iii) safe RL with control barrier functions / reachability shields; (iv) perception-aware MPC for quadrotor obstacle avoidance; gap: no unified framework where RL learns both a risk-sensitive terminal/value prior and local interaction cost for moving obstacles while MPC remains the certified real-time safety layer.  

Read rationale: inspect representative control architectures to isolate reusable components and missing guarantees.  
```action read
papers:
  - title: "Learning-based model predictive control for autonomous racing"
    candidates: ["Rosolia & Borrelli; OpenAlex ID not resolved; DOI/venue metadata available in literature"]
  - title: "Deep Drone Racing: From Simulation to Reality with Domain Randomization"
    candidates: ["Loquercio et al.; OpenAlex ID not resolved in simulated pass"]
  - title: "Model Predictive Contouring Control for Time-Optimal Quadrotor Flight"
    candidates: ["Foehn et al.; OpenAlex ID not resolved in simulated pass"]
  - title: "Safe reinforcement learning via shielding/control barrier functions"
    candidates: ["multiple robotics-control papers; OpenAlex IDs not resolved in simulated pass"]
```
[result]: Extracted design motifs: MPC excels at hard input/state constraints and receding-horizon replanning; RL excels at long-horizon priors and perception-conditioned decisions; dynamic clutter requires prediction uncertainty, not just static signed-distance constraints; aggressive flight requires feasibility-aware warm starts and low-latency replanning.  

Genome extraction rationale: formalize prior art as “research genomes” to recombine capabilities without duplicating existing methods.  
```action extract_genome
objects:
  A: "End-to-end agile RL policy"
  B: "Optimization-based quadrotor MPC/MPPI"
  C: "Safety shield / CBF / reachability filter"
  D: "Dynamic-obstacle predictor"
features:
  - role_of_learning
  - constraint_handling
  - dynamic_obstacle_modeling
  - sim_to_real_strategy
  - real_time_feasibility
```
[result]: Genome summary: A has strong perception-to-action adaptation but weak guarantees; B has constraint handling and smooth aggressive trajectories but limited semantic/long-horizon reasoning; C provides last-resort safety but can be myopic/conservative; D improves anticipation but rarely co-trained with the controller. Promising recombination: RL should not directly command thrust; instead it should shape MPC objectives, terminal values, horizon allocation, and uncertainty margins.  

Novelty rationale: compare the recombined design against known hybrids to ensure the proposal is more than “MPC with learned dynamics” or “RL with safety filter.”  
```action novelty_check
idea_sketch: "Risk-aware hierarchical MPC where RL learns latent navigation value, obstacle interaction costs, and adaptive chance-constraint tightening; MPC solves kinodynamic receding-horizon problem; safety backup uses CBF/reachability tube."
baselines:
  - "RL policy + MPC tracker"
  - "MPC with learned residual dynamics"
  - "CBF shielded RL"
  - "MPPI with neural cost"
```
[result]: Novelty assessment: moderately high. Distinguishing elements: bidirectional coupling—MPC rollouts generate counterfactual safety labels for RL, while RL outputs interpretable MPC primitives: terminal value, obstacle-risk field, and adaptive risk budget. The framework targets cluttered dynamic scenes with moving-agent prediction uncertainty and preserves a verifiable fallback controller.  

Synthesis rationale: package the control framework as an implementable study with dataset, metrics, and expected contribution.  
```action propose
```
[result]: final idea plan follows.
{"tool": "propose", "idea_plan": {"Idea": "Risk-Aware Value-Shaped MPC-RL for agile quadrotor navigation: a hierarchical control framework in which reinforcement learning learns perception-conditioned long-horizon navigation value, dynamic-obstacle interaction costs, and adaptive chance-constraint risk budgets, while a real-time nonlinear MPC layer remains responsible for kinodynamic feasibility, actuator limits, collision constraints, and emergency safety fallback.", "ImplementationSteps": {"1": "Build a quadrotor simulation stack with cluttered static obstacles, moving obstacles/agents, onboard-depth or event-camera observations, wind/disturbance randomization, and aggressive reference tasks such as racing gates, moving-window traversal, and target interception.", "2": "Train an RL module that does not output motor commands directly; instead, it outputs MPC-shaping quantities: terminal value estimates, local risk fields around predicted moving obstacles, adaptive horizon/risk allocation, and warm-start trajectory proposals.", "3": "Implement a nonlinear MPC or MPPI controller using the learned quantities as soft guidance while enforcing hard dynamics, thrust/body-rate limits, obstacle chance constraints, and a backup control-barrier or reachability safety filter.", "4": "Create a closed-loop training loop where MPC rollouts produce counterfactual labels for near-collision states, infeasible plans, and conservative detours; use these labels to improve the RL value/risk model through offline-to-online fine-tuning.", "5": "Evaluate against end-to-end RL, RL plus tracking MPC, classical nonlinear MPC, MPPI with hand-designed costs, and CBF-shielded RL in static clutter, dynamic clutter, sensor-noisy, and sim-to-real transfer settings."}, "ImplementationOrder": ["1-2", "2-3", "3-4", "4-5"], "Dataset": "Primary: custom Isaac Sim / Flightmare / Gazebo-based dynamic-clutter quadrotor benchmark with randomized obstacle fields and moving agents. Secondary: EuRoC MAV and UZH-FPV-style real flight logs for perception and dynamics validation, plus real-world motion-capture test flights through moving-obstacle courses.", "EvaluationMetrics": {"success_rate": "Percentage of trials reaching the goal without collision or safety-stop intervention.", "collision_rate": "Number of collisions or minimum-distance violations per kilometer or per episode.", "lap_or_completion_time": "Task completion time for agile navigation, racing, or moving-gate traversal.", "control_feasibility": "Fraction of MPC solves satisfying dynamics, input bounds, and state constraints within the real-time control deadline.", "trajectory_agility": "Peak speed, acceleration, jerk, and body-rate achieved while maintaining safety.", "dynamic_obstacle_clearance": "Minimum and average distance to moving obstacles under prediction uncertainty.", "intervention_rate": "Frequency with which the backup CBF/reachability safety layer overrides the nominal MPC command.", "generalization": "Performance degradation under unseen obstacle layouts, unseen obstacle motion policies, sensor noise, wind, and dynamics mismatch.", "compute_latency": "End-to-end perception-to-control latency and MPC solve time distribution."}, "ExpectedOutcome": "The proposed framework should outperform pure RL in safety and sim-to-real reliability, outperform classical MPC in dynamic clutter and long-horizon decision quality, and outperform simple RL-MPC cascades by using RL to shape interpretable MPC objectives and risk budgets rather than directly commanding the vehicle. The expected contribution is a certifiably safer yet agile quadrotor navigation controller that can fly near dynamic obstacles at high speed while maintaining real-time feasibility."}}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/biology] Compare the roles of BRCA1 and BRCA2 in homologous recombination repair across human and mouse genomes. Respond only as gene_genome.
full_prompt: Compare the roles of BRCA1 and BRCA2 in homologous recombination repair across human and mouse genomes. Respond only as gene_genome.
completion:
To ground the comparison, I first search for foundational and comparative BRCA1/BRCA2 homologous recombination literature spanning human genetics, mouse models, and orthology-aware genome resources.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"BRCA1 BRCA2 homologous recombination repair human mouse orthologs RAD51 PALB2 embryonic lethal mouse models review","discipline":"biology","candidates":[{"title":"BRCA1 and BRCA2: different roles in a common pathway of genome protection","doi":"10.1038/nrc3181","openalex_id":"W2046462477"},{"title":"A role for BRCA1 in homologous recombination in mouse embryonic stem cells","doi":"10.1126/science.281.5379.1107","openalex_id":"W2015969303"},{"title":"BRCA2 is required for repair of chromosomal breaks by homologous recombination","doi":"10.1038/35001669","openalex_id":"W2120655249"},{"title":"PALB2 links BRCA1 and BRCA2 in the DNA-damage response","doi":"10.1016/j.cub.2009.02.018","openalex_id":"W2036416158"}]}
```  
[result]: Retrieved core candidates showing a consensus division of labor: BRCA1 acts upstream in DNA-damage signaling and end resection, whereas BRCA2 acts more directly in RAD51 loading and stabilization of homologous recombination intermediates. Mouse studies are prominent because complete loss of Brca1 or Brca2 causes embryonic lethality, requiring conditional or hypomorphic alleles for tissue-specific repair phenotypes.

I then read the highest-yield sources to extract mechanistic claims that can be compared across species rather than only summarized in human cancer terms.  
```action
{"tool":"read","ids":["W2046462477","W2015969303","W2120655249","W2036416158"],"focus":"roles of BRCA1 and BRCA2 in HR repair, human-mouse conservation, experimental model distinctions"}
```  
[result]: BRCA1 is consistently described as an early HR factor coordinating damage recognition, checkpoint signaling, chromatin remodeling, 5' end resection through partners such as CtIP/RBBP8, and recruitment of PALB2-BRCA2. BRCA2 is consistently described as a recombinase mediator that binds RAD51 through BRC repeats and a C-terminal RAD51-binding region, loads RAD51 onto single-stranded DNA, limits inappropriate RAD51 binding to double-stranded DNA, and protects stalled replication forks. In mouse cells, Brca1 and Brca2 loss recapitulate HR deficiency and genomic instability, but viable analyses often depend on engineered alleles because null mutations are early lethal.

Next I extract the gene/genome layer: ortholog identity, genomic context, domain conservation, and the kinds of annotations needed for a cross-genome comparison.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","genes":["BRCA1","BRCA2"],"species":["Homo sapiens","Mus musculus"],"assembly":["GRCh38","GRCm39"],"features":["orthologs","chromosomal_location","exon_structure","protein_domains","interactors","HR_repair_annotation"]}
```  
[result]: Human BRCA1 is located on chromosome 17q21.31; mouse Brca1 is on chromosome 11 in a conserved syntenic region. Human BRCA2 is located on chromosome 13q13.1; mouse Brca2 is on chromosome 5. Both genes have clear one-to-one orthologs between human and mouse. Conserved functional features include BRCA1 RING and tandem BRCT domains, and BRCA2 BRC repeats plus DNA-binding/RAD51-interaction regions. HR annotations are strongly conserved, but transcript structure, regulatory elements, repetitive context, and some poorly conserved low-complexity regions differ between genomes.

I compare the extracted genome features to prioritize what a medium-scale human–mouse study should test rather than merely annotate.  
```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","reference_species":"Homo sapiens","query_species":"Mus musculus","genes":["BRCA1","BRCA2"],"questions":["Which HR-relevant features are conserved?","Which features may cause species-specific phenotypes?","Which assays distinguish BRCA1 versus BRCA2 function?"]}
```  
[result]: Conserved: pathway order, essentiality for high-fidelity double-strand break repair, BRCA1-PALB2-BRCA2-RAD51 axis, major protein interaction domains, and tumor-suppressive genome-stability roles. Potentially divergent: regulatory architecture, isoform usage, species-specific tolerance of hypomorphic alleles, cell-cycle and developmental context, and interpretation of pathogenic human variants in mouse knock-in models. Distinguishing assays should measure DNA-end resection, PALB2/BRCA2 recruitment, RAD51 focus formation, sister-chromatid exchange/gene conversion, fork protection, and viability after PARP inhibition or crosslinking agents.
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "gene_genome proposes a comparative study of BRCA1/Brca1 and BRCA2/Brca2 in homologous recombination repair across the human GRCh38 and mouse GRCm39 genomes. The central hypothesis is that the core HR division of labor is deeply conserved: BRCA1 acts mainly as an upstream coordinator of DNA-damage signaling, chromatin access, end resection, and PALB2 recruitment, while BRCA2 acts more directly as the RAD51 mediator that enables strand invasion and protects stalled replication forks. The project will explicitly separate conserved protein-domain functions from species-specific genomic regulation and model-dependent phenotypes.\n\nThe first aim will build an orthology-aware gene-genome map for human BRCA1/BRCA2 and mouse Brca1/Brca2, comparing chromosomal context, exon-intron structure, transcript isoforms, conserved domains, and regulatory annotations. Special attention will be given to BRCA1 RING and BRCT domains and BRCA2 BRC repeats, DNA-binding regions, and RAD51-interaction elements, because these features most directly explain shared HR function. This map will identify which human pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants are suitable for mouse knock-in modeling and which fall in regions where mouse-human conservation is weaker.\n\nThe second aim will compare function in matched human and mouse cell systems using assays that distinguish BRCA1 from BRCA2. BRCA1/Brca1 perturbation will be evaluated for DNA-end resection, checkpoint activation, PALB2-BRCA2 recruitment, and chromatin-associated repair signaling. BRCA2/Brca2 perturbation will be evaluated for RAD51 loading, RAD51 focus formation, gene conversion efficiency, replication-fork protection, and sensitivity to PARP inhibitors or DNA crosslinking agents. Parallel assays will make it possible to ask whether apparent species differences reflect altered gene regulation, protein sequence divergence, developmental context, or experimental allele design.\n\nThe expected outcome is a gene-genome framework clarifying that BRCA1 and BRCA2 participate in the same conserved HR pathway but at different mechanistic stages. BRCA1 should emerge as the earlier pathway organizer and licensing factor for accurate repair, whereas BRCA2 should emerge as the core recombinase mediator executing RAD51-dependent strand exchange. By integrating human and mouse genome annotations with functional HR assays, the study will improve interpretation of mouse cancer models, human variant pathogenicity, and therapeutic vulnerabilities associated with BRCA1- versus BRCA2-deficient genomes."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/biology] Explain how auxin gradients regulate lateral root formation in Arabidopsis, including key transporters and transcription factors.
full_prompt: Explain how auxin gradients regulate lateral root formation in Arabidopsis, including key transporters and transcription factors.
completion:
I first looked for the canonical experimental papers that establish auxin maxima and auxin transport as organizing principles in Arabidopsis lateral root development.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "Arabidopsis lateral root formation auxin gradients PIN AUX1 LAX3 ARF7 ARF19 LBD16 IAA14"}
```
[result]: Found core candidates: Benková et al. 2003, Cell, “Local, efflux-dependent auxin gradients as a common module for plant organ formation”; De Smet et al. 2007, Development, “Auxin-dependent regulation of lateral root positioning in the basal meristem”; Swarup et al. 2008, Nature Cell Biology, “The auxin influx carrier LAX3 promotes lateral root emergence”; Okushima et al. 2007, Plant Cell, ARF7/ARF19-LBD regulation of lateral root formation. OpenAlex IDs were not reliably resolved in this simulated pass.

I then extracted the mechanistic themes shared across those studies: where the gradient comes from, how it is read, and how it is translated into founder-cell divisions and emergence.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "candidates": ["Benkova2003_Cell_DOI:10.1016/S0092-8674(03)00924-3", "DeSmet2007_Development_DOI:10.1242/dev.02753", "Swarup2008_NCB_DOI:10.1038/ncb1754", "Okushima2007_PlantCell_ARF7_ARF19_LBD"]}
```
[result]: Auxin response oscillations and local auxin maxima mark prebranch sites and xylem-pole pericycle founder cells. PIN efflux carriers and AUX1/LAX influx carriers establish and reinforce auxin accumulation. Auxin signaling through TIR1/AFB-mediated Aux/IAA degradation releases ARF transcription factors, especially ARF7 and ARF19, which activate LBD/ASL genes required for lateral root initiation. LAX3 later promotes emergence by increasing auxin uptake in overlying tissues and inducing cell-wall-remodeling programs.

{"tool": "propose", "answer": "Auxin gradients regulate Arabidopsis lateral root formation by converting a spatial hormone distribution into a developmental program. Lateral roots originate mainly from xylem-pole pericycle cells. Before visible initiation, auxin-response oscillations in the basal meristem help define “prebranch sites,” and a local auxin maximum then forms in selected pericycle founder cells. This maximum is not simply passive diffusion: it is produced and sharpened by polar auxin transport.\n\nThe key transporters are auxin influx carriers of the AUX1/LAX family and auxin efflux carriers of the PIN family. AUX1 contributes to auxin uptake and rootward/shootward auxin flow, while LAX3 is especially important during lateral root emergence because it promotes auxin accumulation in the cortical and epidermal cells overlying a new primordium. PIN proteins, including PIN1, PIN3, and PIN7, help direct auxin flow and create local maxima in founder cells and developing primordia. These transport systems generate a focused auxin peak at the initiation site and later maintain patterned auxin distribution within the primordium, with high auxin near the emerging tip.\n\nCells read this auxin gradient through the canonical TIR1/AFB–Aux/IAA–ARF signaling pathway. At low auxin, Aux/IAA repressors such as IAA14/SLR inhibit auxin response factors. When auxin accumulates, Aux/IAA proteins are degraded, releasing ARFs. Two central transcription factors are ARF7 and ARF19, which activate LBD/ASL genes such as LBD16, LBD18, and LBD29. These LBD transcription factors promote founder-cell identity, asymmetric divisions, and early lateral root primordium development.\n\nAs the primordium grows, auxin also coordinates its passage through overlying root tissues. LAX3-dependent auxin accumulation in cortex and epidermis induces cell-wall-loosening and remodeling genes, allowing the new lateral root to emerge. Thus, auxin gradients act at several levels: they choose where a lateral root will form, trigger pericycle founder-cell divisions, pattern the developing primordium, and soften overlying tissues for emergence. In short, lateral root formation is controlled by a feedback loop in which polar auxin transport creates local auxin maxima, and auxin-responsive transcription factors convert those maxima into organ initiation and outgrowth."}

topic: [en/idea_plan/neuroscience] Create a mechanistic research plan on how blood-brain barrier disruption contributes to early neurodegeneration in Alzheimer's disease; respond strictly in the
full_prompt: Create a mechanistic research plan on how blood-brain barrier disruption contributes to early neurodegeneration in Alzheimer's disease; respond strictly in the idea_plan schema.
completion:
1. Map clinical evidence that BBB leakage appears before overt dementia and localizes to hippocampal/medial temporal vulnerability.  
```action search {"query":"Alzheimer early blood-brain barrier breakdown hippocampus DCE-MRI CSF soluble PDGFR beta cognition APOE4","discipline":"neuroscience","limit":5} ```  
[result]: Found anchor candidates: Montagne et al., *Neuron* 2015, DOI 10.1016/j.neuron.2015.05.024; Nation et al., *Nature Medicine* 2019, DOI 10.1038/s41591-018-0297-y; Montagne et al., *Nature* 2020, DOI 10.1038/s41586-020-2247-3. OpenAlex IDs were not resolved in simulated cache; DOIs retained for candidate traceability.

2. Read clinical anchor studies to identify measurable early-BBB variables and temporal placement relative to amyloid/tau.  
```action read {"papers":["10.1016/j.neuron.2015.05.024","10.1038/s41591-018-0297-y","10.1038/s41586-020-2247-3"],"extract":["cohorts","BBB measures","pericyte markers","AD biomarker relationship","mechanistic claims"]} ```  
[result]: DCE-MRI Ktrans and CSF soluble PDGFRβ repeatedly implicate hippocampal/capillary BBB breakdown and pericyte injury in aging, MCI, and APOE4-related cognitive decline. BBB dysfunction can associate with cognitive impairment independently of amyloid/tau status, supporting an early mechanistic role rather than a late epiphenomenon.

3. Identify mechanistic BBB-to-neurodegeneration pathways connecting vascular leak to synaptic, glial, and neuronal injury.  
```action search {"query":"mechanisms blood-brain barrier disruption pericyte loss fibrinogen thrombin albumin microglia astrocyte synapse loss Alzheimer's disease","discipline":"neuroscience","limit":8} ```  
[result]: Recurrent mechanisms: pericyte degeneration, APOE4–cyclophilin A–NFκB–MMP9 pathway, tight-junction loss, fibrinogen/fibrin extravasation activating microglia, thrombin/complement signaling, albumin/TGFβ astrocyte activation, impaired Aβ clearance through LRP1/RAGE imbalance, hypoperfusion, oxidative stress, and neurovascular uncoupling.

4. Extract candidate molecular network for experimental targeting and biomarker alignment.  
```action extract_genome {"source":"BBB disruption early AD mechanism papers","entities":["genes","proteins","cell_types","pathways"],"focus":"vascular-to-neuronal degeneration axis"} ```  
[result]: Candidate mechanistic nodes: APOE, LRP1, RAGE/AGER, PDGFRB, MMP9, MMP2, CypA/PPIA, NFκB/RELA, CLDN5, OCLN, TJP1/ZO1, FGA/FGB/FGG fibrinogen chains, thrombin/F2, C3, C1QA, TGFBR1/2, TGFB1, AQP4, VEGFA, ICAM1, VCAM1, IL1B, TNF, TREM2. Cell types: brain endothelial cells, pericytes, astrocytes, microglia, neurons, oligodendrocytes. Pathways: pericyte-endothelial signaling, basement membrane remodeling, leukocyte adhesion, coagulation/complement activation, astrocyte reactivity, microglial synapse pruning, impaired amyloid clearance, neurovascular uncoupling.

5. Compare the candidate pathway against general aging BBB leakage to isolate AD-relevant early-neurodegeneration mechanisms.  
```action genome_diff {"condition_A":"early Alzheimer's disease / APOE4 / amyloid-positive MCI with BBB disruption","condition_B":"non-demented aging with BBB leakage","features":["molecular nodes","cell types","imaging biomarkers","CSF/plasma biomarkers","causal predictions"]} ```  
[result]: Shared aging features: mild hippocampal leak, reduced tight-junction integrity, oxidative stress, vascular stiffness. AD-enriched features: APOE4-associated CypA–MMP9 activation in pericytes/endothelium, impaired LRP1-mediated Aβ clearance, fibrin/complement-driven microglial synapse loss, tau propagation facilitated by inflammatory vascular milieu, stronger coupling of CSF sPDGFRβ and Ktrans with memory decline. High-value causal prediction: pericyte injury and plasma-protein extravasation should precede measurable synapse loss and glial activation in vulnerable hippocampal circuits.

6. Check novelty of an integrated longitudinal-human plus perturbational-model research plan.  
```action novelty_check {"idea":"Test whether early BBB breakdown causally drives hippocampal neurodegeneration through pericyte injury, plasma-protein leakage, microglial/complement synapse loss, and impaired Aβ clearance using longitudinal DCE-MRI/CSF biomarkers plus iPSC BBB-on-chip and APOE-targeted mouse perturbations.","compare_against":["clinical BBB biomarker studies","APOE4 BBB mechanism studies","fibrinogen microglia studies","amyloid clearance studies"]} ```  
[result]: Novelty score: moderate-high. Individual components exist, but the integrated temporal-causal plan linking human early-BBB biomarkers to experimentally perturbable pericyte/APOE/MMP9/fibrin-complement mechanisms and synaptic degeneration endpoints is less common. Strong differentiator: require temporal mediation testing in humans and causal rescue in models using matched biomarkers.

7. Convert findings into a mechanistic SGI-Bench idea_plan.  
```action propose {"schema":"idea_plan","constraints":["mechanistic","early neurodegeneration","Alzheimer's disease","BBB disruption"]} ```

full_prompt: Review the ecological impacts of invasive zebra mussels on freshwater food webs in North America, emphasizing nutrient cycling and native biodiversity.
completion:
I’ll first collect cornerstone synthesis and empirical studies spanning Great Lakes and large-river systems, prioritizing papers that explicitly connect zebra mussels to food-web restructuring, nutrient cycling, and biodiversity loss.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"zebra mussel Dreissena polymorpha North America freshwater food web nutrient cycling native biodiversity review meta-analysis"}
```  
[result]: Key candidates identified: Higgins & Vander Zanden 2010, “What a difference a species makes: a meta-analysis of dreissenid mussel impacts on freshwater ecosystems” (Ecological Monographs, DOI: 10.1890/09-1249.1); Strayer et al. 1999, “Transformation of freshwater ecosystems by bivalves: a case study of zebra mussels in the Hudson River” (BioScience, DOI: 10.2307/1313324); Hecky et al. 2004, “The nearshore phosphorus shunt: a consequence of ecosystem engineering by dreissenids in the Laurentian Great Lakes” (Canadian Journal of Fisheries and Aquatic Sciences, DOI: 10.1139/f04-065); Caraco et al. 1997, “Zebra mussel invasion in a large, turbid river: phytoplankton response to increased grazing” (Ecology, DOI: 10.1890/0012-9658(1997)078[0588:ZMIIAL]2.0.CO;2). OpenAlex IDs were not returned in this simulated search result.

I’ll next extract the major mechanisms and evidence patterns from these sources so the final review can distinguish direct ecosystem-engineering effects from cascading food-web responses.  
```action
{"tool":"read","papers":["Higgins & Vander Zanden 2010","Strayer et al. 1999","Hecky et al. 2004","Caraco et al. 1997"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted themes: zebra mussels strongly reduce phytoplankton through filtration, increase water clarity, redirect production from pelagic to benthic pathways, and alter nutrient regeneration through excretion and biodeposition. Evidence is strongest for declines in suspended algae and native unionid mussels, increases in benthic algae/macrophytes in clearer shallow waters, and shifts in zooplankton and fish diets. Nutrient impacts are context-dependent: dreissenids can retain or recycle phosphorus and nitrogen nearshore, concentrate organic matter in sediments, and contribute to spatial decoupling between offshore nutrient limitation and nearshore benthic enrichment.

I’ll verify that the review emphasis—linking nutrient shunting to biodiversity-mediated food-web change in North America—is sufficiently focused rather than merely duplicating existing dreissenid syntheses.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"A short review emphasizing how zebra mussel nutrient cycling mechanisms reshape North American freshwater food webs and native biodiversity."}
```  
[result]: Novelty assessment: existing reviews cover broad dreissenid impacts, but a concise synthesis centered specifically on nutrient cycling as the mechanism connecting pelagic-benthic food-web shifts and native biodiversity loss remains useful, especially if it separates zebra mussel effects from broader dreissenid/quagga mussel impacts and highlights Great Lakes versus river-system differences.

{"tool":"propose","answer":"This review will synthesize how invasive zebra mussels, Dreissena polymorpha, have altered freshwater food webs in North America by acting as ecosystem engineers. The central argument is that zebra mussels do not simply add a new consumer to lakes and rivers; they redirect energy and nutrients between habitats. By filtering large volumes of water, they reduce phytoplankton biomass, increase water clarity, and weaken pelagic food pathways that support zooplankton and planktivorous fish.\n\nA major focus will be nutrient cycling. Zebra mussels package suspended organic matter into feces and pseudofeces, deposit it on the benthos, and excrete dissolved nutrients such as ammonium and phosphorus. These processes create a benthic-pelagic shift, often described in the Great Lakes as a nearshore phosphorus shunt: nutrients that once supported offshore phytoplankton are retained or recycled in shallow benthic zones. This can stimulate benthic algae, macrophytes, and microbial activity while contributing to offshore nutrient limitation and altered seasonal productivity.\n\nThe review will also assess impacts on native biodiversity. The most severe direct effects occur on native unionid mussels, which are smothered, energetically stressed, and often extirpated after zebra mussel colonization. Indirectly, clearer water and altered primary production can restructure zooplankton communities, benthic invertebrates, fish foraging patterns, and habitat use. Some benthic taxa and visual predators may benefit, but these gains occur within a reorganized food web that often favors tolerant, opportunistic, or nonnative species over historically dominant native assemblages.\n\nThe proposed synthesis will compare evidence from the Laurentian Great Lakes, the Hudson River, and other invaded North American waters to show that zebra mussel impacts are context-dependent but mechanistically consistent. The review will conclude that nutrient cycling is the key bridge between zebra mussel filtration and broader biodiversity change: by moving resources from the water column to the lake or river bottom, zebra mussels transform both the physical environment and the biological relationships that structure freshwater food webs."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/computer_science] Analyze the role of formal verification in improving the security of smart contracts. Discuss representative tools, common vulnerabilities they target, and limi
full_prompt: Analyze the role of formal verification in improving the security of smart contracts. Discuss representative tools, common vulnerabilities they target, and limitations of current verification workflows.
completion:
I’ll first gather representative verification/security-analysis work to anchor the proposal in known tools and vulnerability classes.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"formal verification smart contracts tools vulnerabilities Oyente Securify KEVM Certora SMTChecker VerX","discipline":"computer_science","candidates":[{"title":"Making Smart Contracts Smarter","venue":"ACM CCS 2016","openalex_id":"W2164238029"},{"title":"Securify: Practical Security Analysis of Smart Contracts","venue":"ACM CCS 2018","openalex_id":"W2895297294"},{"title":"KEVM: A Complete Formal Semantics of the Ethereum Virtual Machine","venue":"IEEE CSF 2018"},{"title":"VerX: Safety Verification of Smart Contracts","venue":"IEEE S&P 2020"},{"title":"Finding The Greedy, Prodigal, and Suicidal Contracts at Scale","venue":"ACSAC 2018"}]}
```  
[result]: Found representative strands: symbolic execution tools such as Oyente/Mythril/Manticore; static/datalog-style analyzers such as Securify and Slither; semantics-based verification via KEVM; deductive or SMT-based workflows such as Solidity SMTChecker, Certora Prover, VerX, and theorem-proving approaches in Coq/Isabelle/F*/K.

Next I’ll extract the key technical dimensions: what properties are verified, what vulnerabilities are targeted, and where workflows fail in practice.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","papers":["W2164238029","W2895297294","KEVM-CSF-2018","VerX-SP-2020"],"fields":["tooling_model","target_vulnerabilities","assumptions","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: Common targets include reentrancy, integer overflow/underflow, unchecked calls, authorization errors, transaction-order/timestamp dependence, denial-of-service via gas or failed sends, unsafe delegatecall, and violations of user-defined invariants. Common limitations include path explosion, incomplete environmental modeling, false alarms, mismatch between source-level and bytecode-level semantics, expensive specification writing, difficulty with upgradeable/proxy contracts, and weak coverage of economic/game-theoretic DeFi risks.

{"tool":"propose","answer":"Formal verification improves smart-contract security by shifting analysis from testing a finite set of executions to reasoning about all executions allowed by a formal model. In this setting, a contract is checked against safety properties such as “only the owner can withdraw,” “balances are conserved,” or “no external call can reenter and violate an invariant.” This is especially valuable for blockchain systems because deployed code is public, adversarially accessible, and often difficult or impossible to patch once assets are locked in it.\n\nRepresentative tools occupy different points in the assurance spectrum. Oyente, Mythril, and Manticore use symbolic execution to explore EVM paths and expose bugs such as reentrancy, arithmetic errors, timestamp dependence, and unchecked low-level calls. Securify and Slither perform static analysis to flag common vulnerability patterns at scale. Solidity’s SMTChecker, VerX, and Certora Prover support more property-directed verification, where developers state invariants or rules and SMT solvers try to prove them. KEVM and related K-framework work provide formal EVM semantics, enabling deeper bytecode-level reasoning, while theorem-proving approaches in Coq, Isabelle, or F* can give stronger guarantees for carefully specified systems.\n\nThe main vulnerabilities targeted by these workflows include reentrancy, integer overflow and underflow, authorization failures, transaction-order dependence, timestamp manipulation, gas-related denial of service, unsafe delegatecall or proxy behavior, unchecked external calls, and violations of accounting invariants. In DeFi contracts, verification is increasingly used not only for local code properties but also for protocol-level invariants such as collateralization, conservation of tokens, liquidation safety, or bounded loss under specified market assumptions.\n\nCurrent workflows remain limited by specification quality, scalability, and modeling fidelity. Many tools either generate false positives or miss bugs because they approximate EVM behavior, external contracts, mempool ordering, oracle behavior, or gas costs. Formal proofs are only as strong as the assumptions and properties supplied, and writing those specifications requires expertise that many development teams lack. Upgradeable contracts, cross-contract composability, flash loans, governance mechanisms, and economic attacks are particularly hard to verify because the relevant threat model extends beyond a single contract’s code. Thus, formal verification should be treated as a high-assurance complement to audits, testing, fuzzing, runtime monitoring, and careful protocol design rather than as a complete guarantee of security."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/computer_science] Explore how graph neural networks are used for program analysis tasks such as vulnerability detection, code summarization, or bug localization. Identify methodo
full_prompt: Explore how graph neural networks are used for program analysis tasks such as vulnerability detection, code summarization, or bug localization. Identify methodological strengths, weaknesses, and promising future directions.
completion:
I’ll first identify representative GNN-for-program-analysis work across vulnerability detection, bug localization, and summarization, then synthesize methodological patterns and gaps.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "graph neural networks program analysis vulnerability detection code summarization bug localization Devign GGNN code property graph", "discipline": "computer_science", "candidates": ["Devign: Effective Vulnerability Identification by Learning Comprehensive Program Semantics via Graph Neural Networks", "Learning to Represent Programs with Graphs", "GraphCodeBERT: Pre-training Code Representations with Data Flow", "DeepWukong: Statically Detecting Software Vulnerabilities Using Deep Graph Neural Network", "IVDetect: Vulnerability Detection Based on Intermediate Representations"]}
```

[result]: Found a cluster of key methods: gated graph neural networks over AST/control/data-flow graphs for semantic program representation; code property graph variants for vulnerability detection; graph-enhanced pretraining for code understanding; and hybrid static-analysis-plus-GNN approaches. OpenAlex IDs were not reliably returned for all candidates in this simulated search result, so candidates are named by canonical paper title.

Next I’ll read the core papers most directly tied to GNN methodology rather than general transformer-based code modeling.

```action
{"tool": "read", "papers": ["Learning to Represent Programs with Graphs", "Devign: Effective Vulnerability Identification by Learning Comprehensive Program Semantics via Graph Neural Networks", "DeepWukong: Statically Detecting Software Vulnerabilities Using Deep Graph Neural Network", "GraphCodeBERT: Pre-training Code Representations with Data Flow"], "focus": ["graph construction", "node and edge features", "learning architecture", "task setup", "evaluation limitations"]}
```

[result]: The literature shows a common recipe: construct a graph from code using AST, CFG, DFG, program dependence graph, or code property graph relations; encode tokens, variables, operators, and statements as node features; apply GGNN/GCN/GAT-style message passing; and pool graph or node embeddings for classification, localization, or generation. Strengths include explicit modeling of non-local dependencies, variable-use chains, and control/data-flow relations. Weaknesses include graph extraction fragility, inconsistent benchmarks, limited cross-project generalization, and vulnerability datasets with noisy labels or synthetic patterns.

I’ll now extract the “research genome”: recurring design choices and their consequences.

```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "sources": ["Learning to Represent Programs with Graphs", "Devign", "DeepWukong", "GraphCodeBERT"], "dimensions": ["program representation", "message passing", "task target", "supervision", "interpretability", "scalability", "generalization"]}
```

[result]: Genome extracted:  
1. Representation: AST-only graphs are easy to build but semantically shallow; CFG/DFG/PDG/code-property graphs capture richer semantics but depend on language-specific tooling.  
2. Architecture: GGNNs and GATs support typed-edge propagation, but oversmoothing and limited receptive fields remain issues for large functions.  
3. Task target: vulnerability detection is often graph-level classification; bug localization requires node/line-level attribution; summarization requires graph-to-sequence decoding or graph-enhanced pretraining.  
4. Supervision: many works use labeled vulnerable/non-vulnerable functions, commit diffs, or generated examples, which can introduce confounding artifacts.  
5. Interpretability: attention or saliency can highlight suspicious lines, but faithfulness is uncertain.  
6. Scalability: full interprocedural graphs are costly; most methods truncate to functions or files.  
7. Generalization: models often underperform under project, language, compiler, or coding-style shifts.

I’ll compare current methods against unresolved needs to identify promising future directions.

```action
{"tool": "genome_diff", "baseline": "current GNN-based program analysis", "target": "robust, deployable, semantically faithful program analysis systems", "axes": ["semantic precision", "data quality", "cross-project generalization", "human usability", "integration with static analysis and LLMs"]}
```

[result]: Gaps identified: current systems capture useful structural signals but often lack semantic soundness, robust benchmarks, and deployability. Promising directions include neuro-symbolic pipelines that use static analysis to constrain GNN reasoning; pretraining on multi-relational program graphs; contrastive learning across equivalent code transformations; line-level and path-level explanations validated against developer workflows; and hybrid GNN–LLM systems where GNNs provide semantic structure and LLMs provide natural-language reasoning or summaries.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "Graph neural networks are valuable for program analysis because they make explicit what token-only models often have to infer implicitly: syntactic structure, control flow, data dependencies, variable use-def chains, and program slices. In vulnerability detection, systems such as Devign-style models use graph representations of functions to classify risky code; in bug localization, node- or line-level embeddings can rank suspicious statements; and in code summarization, graph encoders can condition natural-language generation on semantic relationships rather than surface token order alone.\n\nThe main methodological strength of this family is structural bias. GNNs can propagate information along edges that correspond to meaningful program relations, allowing a model to connect a source variable with a distant sink, a condition with a controlled statement, or a definition with later uses. This is especially useful for vulnerabilities and bugs whose evidence is non-local. However, these strengths depend heavily on graph quality. AST-only graphs may miss semantics, while richer program dependence or code property graphs require expensive, language-specific, and sometimes brittle static analysis. Many studies also rely on noisy vulnerability labels, synthetic transformations, or within-project splits that overstate generalization.\n\nA promising research direction is to develop neuro-symbolic graph models for program analysis. Static-analysis tools should construct typed, semantically grounded graphs, while GNNs learn probabilistic patterns over those graphs. Rather than replacing traditional analyzers, GNNs could prioritize warnings, infer likely missing edges, or identify suspicious graph neighborhoods. This direction should be paired with stronger evaluation: cross-project, cross-version, and cross-language splits; tests against semantics-preserving transformations; and line-level explanation benchmarks that measure whether highlighted nodes correspond to real developer fixes.\n\nAnother high-value direction is hybrid GNN–LLM program understanding. GNNs can encode precise structural dependencies, while language models can exploit naming conventions, comments, documentation, and natural-language reasoning. For vulnerability detection, a graph encoder could produce compact semantic evidence that an LLM explains or verifies. For summarization, graph embeddings could ground generated summaries in actual data-flow and control-flow behavior. The central research goal should be not merely higher benchmark accuracy, but robust, explainable, and developer-usable systems that combine the rigor of program analysis with the adaptability of learned representations."}
```

Next I read the mechanism-focused papers to extract the operational pattern: where clipping happens, where noise is added, and what privacy unit is protected.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["arXiv:1712.07557","doi:10.1145/2976749.2978318","doi:10.1145/3133956.3133982","doi:10.1561/2200000083"],"focus":"mechanisms for DP in FL, privacy accounting, and interaction with secure aggregation"}
```

[result]: Key findings: DP-FL commonly clips each client update to bound sensitivity, aggregates many clipped updates, and adds Gaussian noise either at the server or distributed among clients. Privacy can target records/examples, users/clients, or local messages. Secure aggregation hides individual updates from the server but does not by itself bound inference from the final aggregate, so it is often paired with DP. Privacy accounting typically relies on subsampling amplification and composition over rounds, but assumptions about client participation and adversarial observation are crucial.
I then extracted a compact “research genome” of the area: recurring design choices, objectives, and tensions that distinguish variants.

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","sources":["arXiv:1712.07557","doi:10.1145/2976749.2978318","doi:10.1145/3133956.3133982","doi:10.1561/2200000083"],"dimensions":["privacy_unit","trust_model","noise_location","accounting_method","utility_cost","systems_constraint","attack_surface"]}
```

[result]: Genome extracted: (1) privacy unit: example-level vs client-level vs local message-level; (2) trust model: trusted curator/server, semi-honest aggregator with secure aggregation, or no trusted party under local DP; (3) noise location: central server noise, distributed noise, or per-client local randomization; (4) accounting: moments/RDP/GDP-style composition with subsampling assumptions; (5) utility costs: accuracy degradation from clipping/noise, worse for small cohorts and non-IID data; (6) systems constraints: partial participation, dropouts, device heterogeneity, bandwidth, and energy; (7) attack surface: gradient inversion, membership inference, poisoning, collusion, and privacy leakage through adaptively chosen rounds.
To clarify trade-offs, I compared central DP, distributed DP with secure aggregation, and local DP as competing design families rather than treating “DP-FL” as one method.

```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","families":["central_or_client_level_DP_FL","distributed_DP_with_secure_aggregation","local_DP_FL"],"criteria":["trust","noise_scale","robustness","accuracy","deployment_complexity","failure_modes"]}
```

[result]: Central/client-level DP offers the best utility for a fixed privacy budget but assumes a trusted aggregator or at least trusted noise addition. Distributed DP plus secure aggregation reduces trust in the server and can hide individual updates, but requires cryptographic protocols, dropout-tolerant noise generation, and protection against collusion or malicious clients withholding noise. Local DP has the weakest trust assumptions because each client randomizes before sending, but its noise burden is high, often hurting model accuracy sharply in high-dimensional deep learning. Across all families, heterogeneous data and repeated participation complicate privacy accounting and can make nominal guarantees misleading if the participation process is not modeled correctly.
Finally I checked which questions are still open enough to warrant a research proposal rather than a textbook synthesis.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claims":["DP-FL still lacks robust accounting under realistic partial participation and adaptive training","privacy-utility trade-offs are strongly shaped by non-IID data and client sampling","secure aggregation plus distributed noise is promising but fragile under dropout/collusion","formal DP guarantees do not fully address poisoning, gradient leakage, or implementation side channels"],"discipline":"computer_science"}
```

[result]: Novelty check: The claims are consistent with current literature and remain active research problems. Especially underexplored are end-to-end guarantees that jointly model client sampling, dropout, heterogeneous contribution sizes, adaptive hyperparameters, secure aggregation failures, and adversarial clients. A concise synthesis should emphasize that DP is a formal leakage bound, not a complete privacy/security solution.
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "Differential privacy in federated learning is usually applied by bounding each participant’s influence on the global model. The standard mechanism is to clip client updates, aggregate many clipped updates, and add calibrated random noise before releasing the aggregate or updated model. This can protect individual records or entire clients, with client-level DP being more natural for federated systems because a user’s full local dataset may be sensitive. Privacy loss is tracked over many training rounds using composition and subsampling-based accounting.\n\nThe main design choice is the trust model. In central DP, the server aggregates updates and adds noise, giving relatively strong utility but requiring trust in the server. In local DP, each client perturbs its update before sending it, reducing trust assumptions but usually adding so much noise that accuracy suffers, especially for high-dimensional neural networks. A middle path combines secure aggregation with distributed noise: the server sees only a noisy aggregate, not individual updates, but the system must handle dropouts, collusion, and malicious clients that may fail to contribute noise.\n\nThe central trade-off is privacy versus model quality, but in FL this trade-off is intensified by non-IID data, partial participation, small client cohorts, communication limits, and repeated user participation. Stronger privacy requires more clipping and noise, which can erase rare but useful signals and disproportionately harm minority or underrepresented clients. DP can also interact poorly with robustness: noise may obscure poisoned updates, while clipping may limit both attacks and legitimate contributions.\n\nUnresolved problems include realistic privacy accounting under adaptive training, client sampling, and dropout; reliable distributed noise generation without excessive trust; better methods for private training on highly heterogeneous data; and guarantees that combine DP with defenses against gradient inversion, membership inference, poisoning, and side channels. The key lesson is that DP provides a formal bound on information leakage from released aggregates or models, but it is not a complete privacy architecture unless paired with secure aggregation, careful systems design, auditing, and threat-model-specific robustness."}

completion:

[result]: Candidates include Moore & Read, “Nonabelions in the fractional quantum Hall effect”; Read & Green, “Paired states of fermions in two dimensions with breaking of parity and time-reversal symmetries”; Nayak et al., “Non-Abelian anyons and topological quantum computation” (OpenAlex candidate: W1974792098); Alicea, “New directions in the pursuit of Majorana fermions in solid state systems” (OpenAlex candidate: W2017657684); Mourik et al., “Signatures of Majorana fermions in hybrid superconductor-semiconductor nanowire devices” (OpenAlex candidate: W2088445107); Banerjee et al., “Observation of half-integer thermal Hall conductance” (OpenAlex candidate: W2801003585).

I’ll now extract the comparative claims that distinguish theoretical maturity from experimental evidence in the two platforms.
```action
{"tool":"read","candidates":["Moore-Read 1991","Read-Green 2000","Nayak RMP 2008","Alicea RPP 2012","Mourik Science 2012","Banerjee Nature 2018"]}
```
[result]: Fractional quantum Hall theory gives intrinsically topologically ordered non-Abelian phases, especially the Moore–Read/Pfaffian family at filling factor 5/2, with quasiparticle braiding statistics encoded in conformal field theory and topological quantum field theory. Experiments have reported interferometric hints, tunneling exponents, shot-noise charge measurements, and thermal Hall data consistent with non-Abelian order, but disorder, edge reconstruction, Landau-level mixing, and competition among Pfaffian, anti-Pfaffian, and PH-Pfaffian orders complicate interpretation. Topological-superconductor theory maps engineered spin-orbit-coupled, proximitized, or magnetic systems to effective p-wave superconductors hosting Majorana zero modes. Experiments have observed zero-bias peaks, Coulomb-blockade signatures, Josephson anomalies, and recent device-level progress, but unambiguous non-Abelian braiding and topological protection remain incompletely demonstrated.

I’ll check the novelty angle: comparing the two fields not as rival platforms, but as different evidentiary regimes for the same non-Abelian target.
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"Compare FQH and topological-superconductor routes by separating theoretical completeness, experimental signatures, and evidence needed for non-Abelian braiding."}
```
[result]: The comparison is established but still valuable if framed around evidentiary standards. FQH systems have stronger intrinsic many-body topological theory but harder manipulation and readout; topological superconductors offer more engineerable device geometries but face ambiguity from disorder and trivial Andreev bound states. A concise proposal should emphasize convergence: interferometry, fusion-rule tests, braiding protocols, thermal transport, and scalable qubit architectures.

{"tool":"propose","answer":"A useful comparison should begin by noting that fractional quantum Hall systems supplied the earliest and most internally complete theoretical setting for non-Abelian anyons. The Moore–Read Pfaffian state and related candidates at filling factor 5/2 predict quasiparticles whose fusion and braiding are non-Abelian, with the theory supported by conformal field theory, trial wavefunctions, and topological quantum field theory. The main theoretical uncertainty is not whether such phases can host non-Abelian anyons, but which competing order—Pfaffian, anti-Pfaffian, PH-Pfaffian, or other nearby phases—is realized in actual samples.\n\nExperimentally, fractional quantum Hall evidence is suggestive but not yet definitive. Charge measurements, tunneling, interferometry, and especially thermal Hall conductance have provided important clues consistent with non-Abelian order, but interpretation is complicated by edge reconstruction, disorder, finite temperature, Landau-level mixing, and sample dependence. The great strength of the FQH route is that non-Abelian statistics would arise from an intrinsic many-body topological phase; its weakness is that quasiparticles are difficult to localize, move, and braid in a controlled quantum-information setting.\n\nTopological superconductors followed a different trajectory. Theoretically, they translate the Read–Green p-wave superconductor idea into engineered platforms such as semiconductor-superconductor nanowires, magnetic atom chains, and proximitized two-dimensional systems. These systems are attractive because Majorana zero modes can in principle be created, coupled, measured, and braided in device architectures. However, the theoretical description depends more heavily on material engineering assumptions—spin-orbit coupling, induced pairing, Zeeman fields, disorder control, and hard superconducting gaps.\n\nThe experimental progress in topological superconductors is therefore more device-oriented but also more ambiguous. Zero-bias conductance peaks, Coulomb-blockade effects, and Josephson signatures are compatible with Majorana physics, yet many can be mimicked by trivial Andreev bound states or disorder-induced states. In short, FQH systems currently have the cleaner intrinsic topological theory and some strong bulk/edge evidence, while topological superconductors offer a more scalable manipulation platform but need sharper proof of topological protection and non-Abelian braiding. The decisive next steps in both fields are fusion-rule measurements, interferometric or braiding demonstrations, and reproducible protocols that distinguish true non-Abelian anyons from look-alike phenomena."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/computer_science] Survey recent approaches to retrieval-augmented generation for reducing hallucinations in large language models. Compare their assumptions, evaluation methods,
full_prompt: Survey recent approaches to retrieval-augmented generation for reducing hallucinations in large language models. Compare their assumptions, evaluation methods, and open research challenges.
completion:
I first map the literature around “RAG for hallucination reduction” into seed, recent-method, and evaluation clusters, because the question asks for assumptions, methods, evaluation, and challenges rather than a single model family.

[result]: Candidate set found: Lewis et al., “Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks” (NeurIPS 2020; OpenAlex ID not confidently resolved); Borgeaud et al., “Improving Language Models by Retrieving from Trillions of Tokens” / RETRO (ICML 2022; OpenAlex unresolved); Izacard et al., “Atlas: Few-shot Learning with Retrieval Augmented Language Models” (JMLR/ICML-era 2023; OpenAlex unresolved); Nakano et al., “WebGPT” (2021/2022; OpenAlex unresolved); Gao et al., “RARR: Researching and Revising What Language Models Say, Using Language Models” (ACL 2023; OpenAlex unresolved); Jiang et al., “FLARE: Forward-Looking Active REtrieval Augmented Generation” (2023; OpenAlex unresolved); Asai et al., “Self-RAG: Learning to Retrieve, Generate, and Critique through Self-Reflection” (ICLR 2024; OpenAlex unresolved); Yan et al., “Corrective Retrieval Augmented Generation” / CRAG (2024; OpenAlex unresolved); Es et al., “RAGAS: Automated Evaluation of Retrieval Augmented Generation” (2023; OpenAlex unresolved); Gao et al., “ALCE: Enabling Large Language Models to Generate Text with Citations” (2023; OpenAlex unresolved); Chen et al., “RGB: Benchmarking Large Language Models in Retrieval-Augmented Generation” (2023; OpenAlex unresolved); Min et al., “FActScore: Fine-grained Atomic Evaluation of Factual Precision in Long Form Text Generation” (EMNLP 2023; OpenAlex unresolved). No candidate OpenAlex W-IDs are asserted where not available from the simulated resolver.  

I next read the canonical and architectural papers to identify what each approach assumes is wrong when a model hallucinates: missing knowledge, stale parameters, poor retrieval, poor grounding, or weak self-verification.  
```action
{"tool":"read","ids":["Lewis2020_RAG","Borgeaud2022_RETRO","Izacard2023_Atlas","Nakano2021_WebGPT","Asai2024_SelfRAG","Jiang2023_FLARE","Gao2023_RARR","Yan2024_CRAG"],"focus":"hallucination-reduction assumptions and mechanisms"}
```  
[result]: Core mechanisms extracted: classic RAG treats hallucination as a knowledge-access problem and conditions generation on retrieved passages; RETRO/Atlas scale retrieval or retrieval-pretraining so factual recall is offloaded to an external corpus; WebGPT-style systems assume browsing plus citations can improve answer verifiability; FLARE assumes hallucinations often emerge mid-generation and uses low-confidence future tokens to trigger active retrieval; RARR assumes generated claims can be post-hoc researched and revised; Self-RAG learns when to retrieve and critique its own answer using reflection tokens; CRAG adds retrieval-quality assessment and fallback/correction when retrieved evidence is noisy or irrelevant.  

I then read evaluation-focused work, because hallucination reduction in RAG is often claimed using heterogeneous metrics that are not directly comparable.  
```action
{"tool":"read","ids":["Gao2023_ALCE","Es2023_RAGAS","Chen2023_RGB","Min2023_FActScore","Liu2023_Attribution","Honovich2022_QAFactEval","Thorne2018_FEVER"],"focus":"benchmarks metrics faithfulness attribution factuality citation support"}
```  
[result]: Evaluation families identified: task accuracy on knowledge-intensive QA; citation precision/recall and answer-citation support as in ALCE-like settings; component-level RAG metrics such as context precision, context recall, faithfulness, and answer relevance as in RAGAS; stress tests for insufficient, noisy, contradictory, or irrelevant retrieval as in RGB-style benchmarks; atomic factuality metrics such as FActScore for long-form generation; NLI/QA-based attribution metrics inherited from FEVER, QAFactEval, and related factual consistency work. Human evaluation remains common for citation usefulness, answer correctness, and unsupported claims, but is expensive and inconsistently calibrated.  

To make the comparison systematic, I extract a “method genome”: what is retrieved, when retrieval occurs, how evidence is used, whether verification is learned or prompted, and how success is measured.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","papers":["RAG","RETRO","Atlas","WebGPT","RARR","FLARE","Self-RAG","CRAG","RAGAS","ALCE","RGB","FActScore"],"dimensions":["retrieval_timing","retrieval_unit","controller","evidence_binding","training_signal","failure_mode","evaluation"]}
```  
[result]: Genome summary:  
- Static/pre-generation RAG: retrieve once or per query; assumes retrieved top-k contains sufficient evidence; evaluated with QA accuracy, EM/F1, factuality, citation support.  
- Token/chunk-level retrieval models: retrieve during training/inference at fine granularity; assume scale and locality improve factual continuation; evaluated by perplexity, downstream QA, factual accuracy.  
- Agentic/web RAG: search iteratively, browse, cite, and synthesize; assumes external verification and source selection reduce unsupported claims; evaluated by human preference, citation support, answer correctness.  
- Active/adaptive RAG: retrieve only when uncertainty or anticipated content demands it; assumes unnecessary retrieval can distract while missing retrieval causes hallucination; evaluated by accuracy plus retrieval efficiency and robustness.  
- Post-hoc revise/verify RAG: generate first, decompose claims, retrieve evidence, revise unsupported spans; assumes detection and repair can be separated from generation; evaluated by factuality gains, edit quality, and unsupported-claim reduction.  
- Self-reflective RAG: train models to emit retrieve/critique/support signals; assumes learned process control can outperform fixed pipelines; evaluated by accuracy, citation precision, critique correctness, and ablations on reflection tokens.  

I compare the genomes to expose where claims are not commensurate: some methods reduce errors by improving evidence access, others by making unsupported output observable, and others by changing the generation policy.  
```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","baseline":"static_topk_RAG","comparators":["RETRO","Atlas","WebGPT","RARR","FLARE","Self-RAG","CRAG"],"criteria":["assumptions","retrieval_noise_handling","faithfulness_mechanism","cost_latency","evaluation_validity"]}
```  
[result]: Differences found: RETRO/Atlas emphasize parametric-plus-nonparametric memory at scale but do not by themselves guarantee attribution. WebGPT and ALCE-style citation systems make answers auditable but citation presence can mask unsupported synthesis. FLARE and related active retrieval improve recall for emerging information needs but rely on uncertainty signals that are poorly calibrated in LLMs. RARR improves factuality after generation but can preserve framing errors or over-edit correct claims if retrieval is weak. Self-RAG integrates retrieve/generate/critique decisions but requires supervision or synthetic process labels whose transfer is uncertain. CRAG explicitly models retrieval failure and correction but adds pipeline complexity and depends on reliable retrieval-quality scoring. Static RAG remains simple and strong but is brittle under corpus gaps, adversarial noise, temporal drift, and multi-hop questions.  

Before proposing a synthesis, I check whether the comparative frame itself is novel enough: focusing on “hallucination reduction” requires separating answer correctness, grounding, attribution, and trustworthiness.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"A survey-style synthesis comparing RAG hallucination-reduction methods by assumptions, evaluation designs, and open challenges, with emphasis on mismatch between retrieval availability, evidence faithfulness, and metric validity.","corpus":"computer_science_rag_surveys_2023_2025"}
```  
[result]: Novelty assessment: medium. Existing surveys cover RAG taxonomies, retrieval architectures, and evaluation frameworks, but fewer organize hallucination-reduction approaches by their implicit causal theory of hallucination and by the validity of their evaluation evidence. Suggested contribution: produce a critical synthesis that distinguishes knowledge insufficiency, retrieval failure, evidence misuse, generation overconfidence, and evaluation blind spots; then recommend benchmark protocols that jointly test correctness, attribution, robustness, calibration, and cost.  

I now consolidate the findings into the requested comparative research proposal.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This survey will examine recent retrieval-augmented generation approaches for reducing hallucinations in large language models by organizing them around their implicit theory of why hallucinations occur. Early and still widely used RAG systems assume that hallucination is largely a knowledge-access problem: if the model is given relevant passages at generation time, it should produce more factual answers. Scaling-oriented systems such as RETRO- and Atlas-style models extend this idea by integrating retrieval more deeply into training or inference. More recent approaches modify the assumption: WebGPT- and citation-based systems treat hallucination as a problem of unverifiable synthesis; FLARE-like active retrieval treats it as a failure to retrieve at the right moment; RARR-like systems treat it as a repairable post-generation defect; Self-RAG treats it as a process-control problem in which the model must learn when to retrieve, generate, and critique; and corrective RAG systems treat hallucination as often caused by noisy or insufficient retrieval itself.\n\nThe comparison will evaluate these families along several axes: retrieval timing, evidence granularity, retrieval-quality control, generation-time grounding, post-hoc verification, supervision requirements, and cost. Static top-k RAG is simple and effective but assumes that the corpus is complete and that top-ranked passages are sufficient. Iterative and active retrieval relax this assumption but introduce latency and controller-error risks. Self-reflective and corrective systems are more robust to uncertainty and noisy contexts, but depend on reliable confidence estimates, critique signals, or synthetic process labels. Citation-oriented systems improve auditability, yet citations do not guarantee that every generated claim is entailed by the cited source. Post-hoc revision can substantially reduce unsupported claims, but may fail when the first draft frames the answer incorrectly or when retrieval reinforces a false premise.\n\nThe survey will also compare evaluation methods, because reported hallucination reductions are often not directly comparable. Common evaluations include knowledge-intensive QA accuracy, exact match and F1; citation precision, citation recall, and source support as in citation-generation settings; RAG component metrics such as context precision, context recall, faithfulness, and answer relevance; robustness benchmarks that inject irrelevant, contradictory, or incomplete retrieved passages; and fine-grained factuality measures such as atomic-claim scoring. Human evaluation remains important for long-form answers and citation usefulness, but it is costly and varies across studies. A key argument of the survey will be that factual correctness, faithfulness to retrieved evidence, attribution quality, and user trust are related but distinct outcomes, so a method can improve one while degrading another.\n\nThe main open challenges are to build RAG systems that know when not to answer, detect missing or misleading evidence, handle temporal change, resist adversarial or low-quality retrieved content, and support multi-hop synthesis without fabricating links between sources. Evaluation must move beyond single-dataset accuracy toward protocols that jointly measure correctness, evidence entailment, calibration, abstention, robustness, and cost. The proposed survey will therefore recommend a standardized evaluation matrix: test each RAG method under sufficient, insufficient, noisy, contradictory, and stale-retrieval conditions; score both answer-level factuality and claim-level attribution; report latency and retrieval budget; and include calibration or abstention metrics. This framing should clarify which hallucination problems current RAG methods actually solve and where future research should focus."}
```

[result]: Classical protocols assume compact ephemeral public keys, compact signatures, mature certificate tooling, and stable MTU behavior. {"tool": "propose", "answer": "This research should examine how post-quantum cryptographic algorithms affect existing Internet security protocols by treating migration as a protocol, deployment, and governance problem rather than merely an algorithm replacement exercise. The study would focus on TLS 1.3 and QUIC, WebPKI and X.509 certificate chains, IPsec/IKEv2, SSH, and DNSSEC, with ML-KEM as the leading near-term key-establishment mechanism and ML-DSA/SLH-DSA as the principal standardized signature options. It should also analyze protocol-specific issues: TLS and QUIC handshake growth, QUIC anti-amplification limits, DNSSEC response size and amplification risk, IKEv2 interoperability for VPNs, SSH extension negotiation, and operational problems such as logging, monitoring, certificate transparency, revocation, and compliance validation. A useful deliverable would be a migration matrix mapping each protocol to required standards updates, implementation changes, deployment blockers, and realistic transition stages.\n\nPerformance analysis should combine microbenchmarks with network-level experiments. CPU costs for ML-KEM-style key exchange may be acceptable or even competitive in many environments, but larger public keys, ciphertexts, and especially signatures change bandwidth, latency, cache, memory, and MTU behavior. The research should measure full handshake size, completion latency, server throughput, packet loss sensitivity, mobile-network behavior, constrained-device impact, certificate-chain size, DNS response behavior, and the cost of hybrid modes that carry both classical and post-quantum material. These results should be compared against classical ECDHE/ECDSA/Ed25519/RSA deployments so operators can understand not only cryptographic security gains but also operational costs.\n\nFurther research is needed in formal security analysis of hybrid protocol composition, compact and deployable PQ certificate formats, PQ-aware certificate compression, side-channel-resistant high-performance implementations, transition strategies for IoT and industrial systems, and large-scale measurement of middlebox and client compatibility. Another important area is migration economics and governance: who must move first, how long hybrid modes should remain, how certificate authorities and browser vendors should coordinate, and how organizations should prioritize systems exposed to harvest-now-decrypt-later attacks. The proposed outcome is a protocol-centered roadmap that helps standards bodies, browser vendors, cloud providers, enterprises, and security engineers migrate to post-quantum Internet security with measurable security benefits and manageable deployment risk."}

full_prompt: Compare the roles of dopamine and serotonin in reinforcement learning and mood regulation, including one key brain circuit for each. Respond only as free_text.
completion:

```action

```

[result]: Found canonical candidates including Schultz, Dayan & Montague on dopaminergic reward prediction error; Daw, Kakade & Dayan on opponent interactions between serotonin and dopamine; Cools, Roberts & Robbins on serotonin and affective control; and reviews on mesolimbic dopamine and dorsal raphe serotonergic projections.

I’ll extract the main circuit-level claims relevant to a short neuroscience comparison.

```action
{"tool": "read", "candidates": ["Schultz Dayan Montague 1997 Science dopamine reward prediction error", "Daw Kakade Dayan 2002 opponent interactions serotonin dopamine", "Cools Roberts Robbins serotonin affective control review"]}
```

[result]: Dopamine neurons in the ventral tegmental area and substantia nigra encode reward prediction errors that update action and state values, especially through projections to ventral and dorsal striatum. Serotonin neurons, especially from the dorsal raphe nucleus, modulate mood, anxiety, patience, punishment sensitivity, and behavioral inhibition through widespread projections to prefrontal cortex, amygdala, hippocampus, and striatum.

{"tool": "propose", "answer": "Dopamine and serotonin both influence reinforcement learning and mood, but they are often associated with different computational and circuit functions. Dopamine is most strongly linked to reward prediction error: phasic dopamine firing increases when outcomes are better than expected and decreases when rewards are omitted. This signal helps update value estimates and supports reinforcement learning, motivation, and action selection.\n\nA key dopamine circuit is the mesolimbic pathway from the ventral tegmental area to the nucleus accumbens. This circuit helps assign motivational value to rewards and reward-predicting cues, supporting approach behavior and habit formation. Dopamine also affects mood indirectly: low dopaminergic function is associated with anhedonia, low motivation, and reduced reward sensitivity in depression.\n\nSerotonin has a broader modulatory role in mood regulation and behavioral control. In reinforcement learning, it is often associated with punishment sensitivity, patience, behavioral inhibition, uncertainty, and the regulation of aversive or delayed outcomes rather than simple reward prediction. In mood regulation, serotonin is central to emotional stability, anxiety control, and depressive symptoms, which is why serotonergic drugs such as SSRIs are widely used in depression and anxiety disorders.\n\nA key serotonin circuit is the dorsal raphe nucleus projection system to the prefrontal cortex, amygdala, and hippocampus. Through this network, serotonin modulates emotional appraisal, stress responses, memory, and top-down control of affect. In short, dopamine is especially important for learning what is rewarding and energizing action toward it, while serotonin is especially important for regulating mood, inhibition, and responses to aversive or uncertain situations."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/neuroscience] What are the main differences between EEG, MEG, and fMRI for studying human cognition, especially in terms of temporal and spatial resolution? Respond only as f
full_prompt: What are the main differences between EEG, MEG, and fMRI for studying human cognition, especially in terms of temporal and spatial resolution? Respond only as free_text.
completion:
I’m gathering concise, review-level evidence on the comparative strengths of EEG, MEG, and fMRI for cognitive neuroscience, focusing on temporal and spatial resolution.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"EEG MEG fMRI temporal spatial resolution human cognition review","discipline":"neuroscience"}
```
[result]: Found review-level sources comparing electrophysiological methods with hemodynamic imaging. Consensus: EEG/MEG measure neural activity directly with millisecond temporal resolution, while fMRI measures blood-oxygen-level-dependent activity indirectly with seconds-scale temporal resolution but better anatomical localization.

I’m extracting the core modality differences relevant to a short neuroscience answer.  
```action
{"tool":"read","sources":["review comparisons of EEG MEG fMRI in cognitive neuroscience","methods summaries on temporal and spatial resolution"]}
```
[result]: EEG: inexpensive, portable, excellent temporal resolution, poorer spatial localization due to skull/scalp signal distortion. MEG: excellent temporal resolution, better source localization than EEG for some cortical sources, but expensive and less sensitive to radial/deep sources. fMRI: high spatial resolution across the whole brain, including deeper structures, but poor temporal resolution because it tracks vascular responses rather than neuronal firing.

{"tool":"propose","answer":"EEG, MEG, and fMRI are all used to study human cognition, but they capture different aspects of brain activity. EEG and MEG measure neural activity more directly: EEG records electrical potentials from the scalp, while MEG records magnetic fields generated by neuronal currents. fMRI measures brain activity indirectly through blood-oxygen-level-dependent changes, so it reflects vascular responses linked to neural activity rather than neural signaling itself.\n\nThe biggest temporal-resolution difference is that EEG and MEG are very fast, typically resolving activity on the order of milliseconds. This makes them well suited for studying rapid cognitive processes such as attention, perception, language comprehension, and decision-making. fMRI is much slower, with temporal resolution usually on the order of seconds, because the hemodynamic response lags behind neural activity.\n\nSpatially, fMRI is usually strongest: it can localize activity to millimeter-scale brain regions and can image deep structures as well as cortex. EEG has relatively poor spatial resolution because electrical signals are blurred by the skull and scalp. MEG often has better spatial localization than EEG for some cortical sources, but it is less sensitive to deep sources and to certain source orientations.\n\nIn short, EEG and MEG are best when timing is crucial, while fMRI is best when anatomical localization is crucial. Many cognitive neuroscience studies combine these methods because EEG/MEG reveal when cognitive processes occur, whereas fMRI helps identify where in the brain those processes are most strongly represented."}
