[result]: Simulated candidates included major reviews and primary studies on innate immune activation, microglial genetic risk, astrocyte reactivity, complement-mediated synapse loss, and NLRP3 inflammasome signaling in Alzheimer’s disease. Candidate records with bibliographic identifiers included: Heneka et al., 2015, Lancet Neurology, DOI 10.1016/S1474-4422(15)70016-5; Heppner et al., 2015, Nature Reviews Neuroscience, DOI 10.1038/nrn3880; Hong et al., 2016, Science, DOI 10.1126/science.aad8373; Ising et al., 2019, Nature, DOI 10.1038/s41586-019-1769-z; OpenAlex work IDs were not available in the simulated result.
{"tool":"propose","answer":"Current evidence supports neuroinflammation as an active contributor to Alzheimer’s disease progression rather than a mere byproduct of amyloid and tau pathology. Human genetics implicates innate immune pathways—especially microglial genes such as TREM2, CD33, CR1, and PLCG2—while postmortem, CSF, PET, and transcriptomic studies show inflammatory activation tracking with plaque burden, tau spread, synaptic injury, and cognitive decline.

Microglia are the most consistently implicated cell type. They cluster around amyloid plaques, can initially help compact or clear amyloid, but chronic activation is associated with release of cytokines, complement factors, reactive oxygen species, and inflammasome signaling that may promote synapse loss and tau pathology. Experimental models further suggest that NLRP3 inflammasome activation and complement-mediated pruning can accelerate neurodegenerative changes.

Astrocytes are also strongly implicated. Reactive astrocytes become prominent around plaques and injured tissue, alter glutamate and metabolic support, secrete inflammatory mediators, and interact with microglia to amplify damaging immune states. Additional contributors include peripheral monocytes/macrophages, endothelial cells, and oligodendrocyte-lineage cells, but the strongest evidence centers on dysregulated microglia–astrocyte crosstalk as a driver of disease progression.

Overall, the field now views neuroinflammation as stage-dependent: potentially protective early, but maladaptive when sustained. This helps explain why anti-inflammatory strategies have shown mixed results and suggests that future therapies should target specific immune pathways, disease stages, and cell states rather than broadly suppressing inflammation."}


[result]: The question remains active. Current evidence supports bidirectional hippocampal–entorhinal interactions, but the causal division of labor between grid cells, place cells, sensory landmarks, path integration, and task demands is unresolved, especially in complex or changing environments.

{"tool": "propose", "answer": "Hippocampal place cells and entorhinal grid cells are two major neural systems that support spatial navigation. Place cells, found in the hippocampus, fire when an animal is in a particular location in an environment. Different place cells are active in different locations, so a population of them can form a map-like representation of where the animal is. These maps are not just geometric: they can change with context, goals, memories, and landmarks, which makes place cells especially important for linking “where I am” with “what happened here” or “where I need to go.”\n\nGrid cells, found mainly in the medial entorhinal cortex, work differently. A single grid cell fires at many locations that form a repeating hexagonal pattern across space. This regular pattern is thought to provide a metric or coordinate-like system for navigation. Grid cells are especially useful for path integration: keeping track of position by using self-motion signals such as speed, direction, and distance traveled, even when external landmarks are limited.\n\nTogether, these systems appear to provide complementary parts of a navigation system. Grid cells may supply a structured spatial metric, while place cells may use that information along with sensory landmarks, environmental context, and memory to represent specific locations. In simple terms, grid cells help estimate position within a coordinate framework, while place cells help identify meaningful places within a particular environment.\n\nOne open research question is exactly how these two systems interact when navigation becomes difficult or ambiguous. For example, if an animal’s self-motion cues suggest one position but landmarks suggest another, how does the brain decide which information to trust? More broadly, researchers are still trying to determine how grid-cell path-integration signals, hippocampal place maps, sensory landmarks, and goals are combined during flexible navigation in changing real-world environments."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/physics] Assess the feasibility of using muon tomography to detect hidden voids in large geological structures. Respond only as free_text.
full_prompt: Assess the feasibility of using muon tomography to detect hidden voids in large geological structures. Respond only as free_text.
completion:
I first map the literature to distinguish proven demonstrations from geological-scale feasibility claims.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"cosmic ray muon tomography geological structures void detection feasibility volcano pyramid cave tunnel density contrast","discipline":"physics","k":6}
```
[result]: Simulated search result: Key candidate papers include Morishima et al., “Discovery of a big void in Khufu’s Pyramid by observation of cosmic-ray muons,” Nature 2017, DOI: 10.1038/nature24647; Tanaka et al., muon radiography studies of volcano interiors and lava domes, Earth and Planetary Science Letters / related geophysics venues; Lesparre et al., “Geophysical muon imaging: feasibility and limits,” Geophysical Journal International 2010, DOI: 10.1111/j.1365-246X.2010.04790.x; Marteau et al. and Jourde et al. on muon imaging of volcanoes and hydrological density changes; and review literature on muography for civil engineering, mining, and geosciences. OpenAlex W-identifiers were not reliably returned in this simulated pass, so candidates are tracked by title/DOI where available.

I then read the best-known proof-of-principle cases to extract what has actually been demonstrated.  
```action
{"tool":"read","candidates":["Morishima et al. 2017 Nature DOI:10.1038/nature24647","Lesparre et al. 2010 GJI DOI:10.1111/j.1365-246X.2010.04790.x","Tanaka volcano muography studies"],"focus":"detector size, exposure time, overburden, void scale, statistical confidence, geological applicability"}
```
[result]: Simulated reading result: Muography has detected density anomalies in large man-made and geological structures when the anomaly subtends a measurable solid angle and reduces integrated opacity along the muon path. The Khufu Pyramid result used multiple detector technologies and months-scale exposure to detect a large hidden void in a structure with relatively well-known geometry. Volcano studies demonstrate that cosmic-ray muons can image density contrasts over hundreds of meters to kilometer-scale path lengths, but angular acceptance, low high-energy muon flux, topographic uncertainty, and inversion non-uniqueness limit resolution. Feasibility is therefore case-specific rather than generic.

Next I search for quantitative design rules, because feasibility depends more on opacity and counting statistics than on the word “void.”  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"muography opacity water equivalent muon flux exposure time angular resolution geological feasibility Lesparre","k":5}
```
[result]: Simulated search result: Feasibility is commonly parameterized by opacity, usually expressed as density-length in g cm^-2 or meters water equivalent; detector acceptance in cm^2 sr; exposure time; angular binning; background rejection; and prior knowledge of topography/density. A useful first-order criterion is that a void must produce a statistically significant excess in transmitted muons relative to the expected flux through intact rock, after accounting for detector efficiency, scattering, and open-sky calibration.

I formalize the feasibility variables into a reusable “genome” of the problem.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","source_set":["muon transmission equations","geophysical muography case studies","void detection use case"],"outputs":["controlling parameters","failure modes","minimum viable experiment"]}
```
[result]: Simulated genome: Controlling parameters are: (1) rock thickness and density, hence opacity; (2) void diameter and shape projected along each muon path; (3) detector area, angular resolution, timing/tracking layers, and acceptance; (4) exposure duration; (5) site access below or beside the target; (6) ability to model topography and background density; and (7) inversion strategy using one or multiple viewpoints. Principal failure modes are too much overburden, too small a void relative to angular bin size, insufficient exposure, unknown heterogeneity masquerading as a void, atmospheric/seasonal flux variations, and unmodeled scattered or upward-going background.

I compare several geological target classes to see where the method is plausible and where it is not.  
```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","cases":[{"target":"karst cave or abandoned mine void beneath hillside","overburden":"50-300 m rock","access":"valley/tunnel side","void_scale":"5-50 m"},{"target":"volcanic conduit or crater density anomaly","overburden":"hundreds of m to >1 km","access":"flanks","void_scale":"tens to hundreds of m"},{"target":"deep hidden chamber inside massive mountain","overburden":">1-2 km rock equivalent","access":"limited","void_scale":"10-100 m"}],"metric":"feasibility"}
```
[result]: Simulated comparison: Highest feasibility occurs for hillside, tunnel, karst, mine, dam, and volcanic-flank settings where detectors can view the target through less than a few hundred meters to about a kilometer water-equivalent opacity and where the expected void is at least comparable to the spatial resolution implied by detector distance and angular binning. Moderate feasibility applies to large volcanic density anomalies because signals may be geologically meaningful even if not uniquely “voids.” Low feasibility applies to small or deep voids inside massive mountains, because the surviving muon flux becomes too low and inversion becomes underconstrained without multiple stations or auxiliary geophysics.

Before proposing a study, I check whether the contribution would be novel relative to existing demonstrations.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"A feasibility framework for hidden void detection in large geological structures using muon tomography, integrating opacity-limited counting statistics, detector siting, and joint inversion with conventional geophysics","against":["pyramid muography","volcano muography","geophysical feasibility papers","civil engineering muography reviews"]}
```
[result]: Simulated novelty result: Novelty is strongest if the work does not merely claim that muography can see voids, but instead develops a decision framework: given topography, expected rock density, candidate void size, detector acceptance, and exposure budget, predict the probability of detection and false-alarm risk. A useful contribution would combine simulation, one controlled field validation, and comparison with gravity or seismic data.

I now synthesize the research trajectory into a proposal.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","answer_length":"3-5 paragraphs","topic":"feasibility of using muon tomography to detect hidden voids in large geological structures"}
```
[result]:  
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "This project will assess when cosmic-ray muon tomography is a practical method for detecting hidden voids in large geological structures such as hillsides, volcanic edifices, karst systems, abandoned mines, and engineered rock masses. The central hypothesis is that muography is feasible for void detection when the expected reduction in opacity produces a statistically significant excess of transmitted muons within a realistic exposure time, but that feasibility drops sharply for small voids, deep targets, poorly constrained density fields, or sites with limited detector access.\n\nThe study will combine forward modeling, detector-performance simulation, and field validation. First, we will build a parameterized model linking rock density, path length, void size, detector area, angular resolution, acceptance, and exposure time to expected muon counts. This model will be used to generate detection-threshold maps for representative geological scenarios, including shallow karst voids, mine galleries, volcanic conduits, and deeper mountain-scale cavities. The analysis will explicitly include atmospheric flux variation, detector efficiency, multiple scattering, topographic uncertainty, and the possibility that natural density heterogeneity could mimic a void.\n\nA controlled field experiment will then be designed for a site where partial ground truth exists, such as a known tunnel, mine void, or karst cavity viewed through tens to hundreds of meters of rock. One or more tracking detectors will be placed at accessible viewing positions, with exposure times chosen from the simulation results. The measured angular muon flux will be inverted into opacity anomalies and compared with independent information from gravity, electrical resistivity, seismic surveys, lidar topography, or borehole data. This will test not only whether a void can be detected, but also how accurately its location, size, and confidence level can be recovered.\n\nThe expected outcome is a feasibility framework rather than a universal endorsement of muon tomography. The project should identify regimes where muography is highly promising—large voids, moderate overburden, favorable detector geometry, and strong prior topographic constraints—as well as regimes where it is likely impractical, such as small cavities embedded deep inside kilometer-scale rock masses. Such a framework would help physicists, geologists, civil engineers, and hazard-assessment teams decide when muon tomography is worth deploying and when conventional geophysical methods or joint inversion are required."}

full_prompt: Design a hypothetical experiment using optogenetics to test whether a specific amygdala-to-prefrontal cortex pathway regulates fear extinction in mice.
completion:

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "basolateral amygdala infralimbic prefrontal cortex fear extinction optogenetics mice", "candidates": ["Peters et al., 2009/2010 extinction circuitry reviews", "Senn et al., 2014 long-range amygdala pathways and fear", "Burgos-Robles et al., 2017 amygdala inputs to prefrontal cortex guide behavior"]}
```  
[result]: Simulated search found convergent evidence that infralimbic medial prefrontal cortex activity supports extinction expression, while basolateral amygdala projections are anatomically and functionally positioned to influence prefrontal extinction-related plasticity.

I then narrowed the experiment to a causal, projection-specific optogenetic test rather than broad regional manipulation.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "claim": "Projection-specific optogenetic inhibition or activation of basolateral amygdala terminals in infralimbic medial prefrontal cortex during extinction training will test whether this pathway regulates fear extinction."}
```  
[result]: Simulated novelty check suggests the design is plausible and experimentally interpretable: pathway-specific terminal manipulation during distinct behavioral epochs can distinguish effects on acquisition of extinction from effects on expression or nonspecific freezing.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "I propose a hypothetical experiment testing whether the basolateral amygdala-to-infralimbic medial prefrontal cortex pathway regulates fear extinction in mice. Adult mice would receive an AAV carrying either ChR2, ArchT/eNpHR, or a fluorophore control under a CaMKIIα promoter into the basolateral amygdala, with optic fibers implanted above infralimbic prefrontal cortex to selectively manipulate BLA axon terminals in IL during behavior.\n\nMice would undergo standard auditory fear conditioning, followed 24 hours later by extinction training in a distinct context with repeated tone presentations without shock. During extinction, one group would receive terminal inhibition of BLA→IL projections during each tone, another would receive terminal activation, and control mice would receive light without opsin or opsin without effective stimulation. Freezing would be measured during conditioning, extinction learning, and a drug-free extinction-recall test the next day.\n\nThe key prediction is that inhibiting BLA→IL terminals during extinction training will impair extinction learning or later extinction recall, producing persistently high freezing, whereas activating the pathway during extinction may accelerate reduction in freezing and improve next-day recall. To distinguish extinction learning from extinction expression, an additional cohort could receive light only during the recall test rather than training; if manipulation affects recall but not learning, freezing changes should appear only during the light-on recall session.\n\nControls would include verification of viral expression and fiber placement, histological confirmation that labeled BLA axons terminate in IL, open-field or tone-reactivity controls to rule out locomotor or sensory effects, and counterbalanced light delivery. If BLA→IL manipulation bidirectionally alters extinction without affecting baseline movement or fear acquisition, the experiment would support a causal role for this amygdala-to-prefrontal pathway in regulating fear extinction."}
```

topic: [en/free_text_answer/materials] Identify emerging recyclable thermoset or vitrimer materials suitable for wind turbine blades. Respond only using the free-text answer schema, with emphasis on
full_prompt: Identify emerging recyclable thermoset or vitrimer materials suitable for wind turbine blades. Respond only using the free-text answer schema, with emphasis on chemistry, processing constraints, and end-of-life pathways.
completion:
I’ll first triangulate recent blade-relevant vitrimer/recyclable thermoset chemistries against known processing and recycling constraints, then screen for novelty gaps rather than mature thermoplastic blade routes.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"recyclable thermoset vitrimer epoxy wind turbine blade composite dynamic covalent end-of-life recycling 2020 2024"}
```  
[result]: Found candidate clusters: (1) epoxy/anhydride or epoxy/acid vitrimers using transesterification catalysts for glass-fiber composites, often compatible with infusion/RTM but challenged by viscosity/catalyst latency and long hot-press reprocessing; (2) disulfide, imine, acetal/ketal, and boronic-ester dynamic epoxy networks with lower-temperature repair or chemical deconstruction but moisture/creep risks; (3) cleavable epoxy systems designed for solvolysis recovery of glass fiber; (4) commercial-adjacent recyclable thermosets and thermoplastics for blades, including CET-like epoxy, Arkema Elium acrylic thermoplastic resin, and Aditya Birla/Siemens Gamesa Recyclamine-type systems. Representative foundational/candidate papers include Leibler’s vitrimer concept paper in Science 2011 (OpenAlex candidate W2157060637 when available), multiple epoxy vitrimer composite studies 2014–2023, and recent wind-blade recycling reviews emphasizing resin infusion, fatigue, Tg, fire/smoke, durability, and fiber recovery economics.  

To avoid proposing a generic “make a vitrimer blade,” I’ll extract the actionable design rules and identify a near-term material family with a differentiating end-of-life pathway.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"A blade-scale recyclable thermoset based on latent-catalyzed epoxy vitrimer chemistry tailored for vacuum infusion, with selective low-energy deconstruction to recover glass fiber and resin oligomers."}
```  
[result]: Novelty is moderate if framed simply as epoxy vitrimer blades; stronger if the proposal couples (i) latency/viscosity control for vacuum-assisted resin infusion, (ii) marine/humidity-stable dynamic linkages, (iii) fatigue/creep testing under blade-relevant load spectra, and (iv) dual end-of-life options: on-site repair/welding and centralized solvolytic/fiber-recovery recycling. Avoid overclaiming full blade recyclability unless adhesive joints, coatings, balsa/PET cores, lightning protection, and mixed-material separation are included.  

{"tool":"propose","answer":"I propose to target a next-generation recyclable blade resin based on a latent-catalyzed epoxy vitrimer rather than a fully thermoplastic blade matrix. The most promising chemistry is a high-Tg epoxy/acid or epoxy/anhydride network containing β-hydroxy ester exchange sites, with a blocked transesterification catalyst that remains inactive during resin storage and infusion but activates during post-cure or recycling. This class preserves the familiar thermoset processing window needed by wind-blade manufacturers—low initial viscosity for vacuum-assisted resin infusion, long pot life, room-temperature or mildly heated layup compatibility, and cure temperatures compatible with thick glass-fiber laminates—while enabling stress relaxation, repair, and reshaping at elevated temperature."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/clinical_medicine] Review the current evidence on SGLT2 inhibitors for patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, including benefits, risks, and patient selecti
full_prompt: Review the current evidence on SGLT2 inhibitors for patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, including benefits, risks, and patient selection. Respond only as free_text.
completion:
I’m gathering the pivotal randomized trial and review evidence most relevant to HFpEF/HFmrEF patient care.

```action
search(query="SGLT2 inhibitors heart failure preserved ejection fraction EMPEROR-Preserved DELIVER meta-analysis benefits risks patient selection", discipline="clinical_medicine")
```

[result]: Key candidates identified: EMPEROR-Preserved, empagliflozin in HF with EF >40%, NEJM 2021, DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2107038; DELIVER, dapagliflozin in HF with mildly reduced or preserved EF, NEJM 2022, DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2206286; pooled/meta-analytic evidence supporting class effect across EF spectrum; guideline updates recommending SGLT2 inhibitors for HFpEF/HFmrEF.

I’m extracting the clinically actionable findings: effect size, safety signals, and which patients were represented.

```action
read(candidates=["EMPEROR-Preserved NEJM 2021", "DELIVER NEJM 2022", "major HF guidelines and meta-analyses"])
```

[result]: EMPEROR-Preserved showed empagliflozin reduced the composite of cardiovascular death or hospitalization for heart failure, mainly through fewer HF hospitalizations. DELIVER showed dapagliflozin similarly reduced worsening HF or cardiovascular death in patients with EF >40%, including many with EF ≥50%. Benefits were consistent regardless of diabetes status. Risks included genital mycotic infections, volume depletion/hypotension, transient eGFR dip, rare ketoacidosis, and need for caution in advanced kidney disease, frailty, low blood pressure, recurrent urinary/genital infections, and perioperative fasting states.

{"tool":"propose","answer":"Current evidence supports SGLT2 inhibitors—especially empagliflozin and dapagliflozin—as disease-modifying therapy for patients with heart failure with preserved or mildly reduced ejection fraction. The strongest data come from EMPEROR-Preserved and DELIVER, which enrolled symptomatic patients with EF generally above 40% and showed significant reductions in the composite of worsening heart failure or cardiovascular death, driven primarily by fewer heart-failure hospitalizations. Benefits appear broadly consistent in patients with and without diabetes, making these drugs heart-failure therapies rather than simply glucose-lowering agents.\n\nThe magnitude of benefit is clinically meaningful but should be framed accurately: SGLT2 inhibitors improve congestion-related outcomes and reduce HF events more consistently than they reduce cardiovascular or all-cause mortality in HFpEF. They also modestly slow kidney function decline and are generally easy to administer as once-daily oral drugs. Their role is particularly attractive because HFpEF has few therapies with randomized trial evidence across a broad population.\n\nRisks are usually manageable but important. Common concerns include genital fungal infections, mild diuresis with volume depletion or hypotension, and an early small fall in eGFR that often stabilizes. Rare but serious adverse events include euglycemic ketoacidosis, especially during acute illness, prolonged fasting, surgery, or marked insulin deficiency. Clinicians should review renal function, blood pressure, diuretic dose, infection history, and sick-day guidance before and after initiation.\n\nPatient selection should prioritize symptomatic HFpEF or HFmrEF patients with evidence of structural heart disease, elevated natriuretic peptides, or prior HF hospitalization, similar to trial populations. SGLT2 inhibitors are especially compelling in patients with recurrent congestion, diabetes, chronic kidney disease within approved eGFR thresholds, or high HF hospitalization risk. Caution is warranted in patients with very low blood pressure, severe volume depletion, advanced kidney dysfunction below labeling thresholds, recurrent genital infections, active ketoacidosis risk, or limited expected benefit due to non-cardiac frailty. Overall, for most eligible HFpEF patients, SGLT2 inhibitors should be considered foundational therapy alongside careful management of blood pressure, atrial fibrillation, obesity, sleep apnea, ischemia, and diuretic needs."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/clinical_medicine] Summarize recent advances in the diagnosis and management of early-onset colorectal cancer, with attention to screening guidelines, genetic evaluation, and trea
full_prompt: Summarize recent advances in the diagnosis and management of early-onset colorectal cancer, with attention to screening guidelines, genetic evaluation, and treatment considerations.
completion:
I’ll first identify recent, clinically relevant guidance and review evidence on early-onset colorectal cancer (EOCRC), prioritizing screening, hereditary-risk assessment, and management implications.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "early-onset colorectal cancer recent advances screening guidelines genetic evaluation treatment 2021 2024 USPSTF NCCN ASCO"}
```

[result]: Found high-relevance sources including: USPSTF 2021 colorectal cancer screening recommendation lowering average-risk screening to age 45; American Cancer Society and multisociety guidance aligning with age-45 initiation; NCCN updates emphasizing germline multigene testing in younger CRC patients; reviews documenting rising rectal/left-sided EOCRC incidence, diagnostic delays, and distinct survivorship needs. Candidate papers/guidelines included DOI-indexed records; OpenAlex IDs were not available in this simulated result.

Next, I’ll extract key clinical themes and distinguish established recommendations from emerging or debated practices.

```action
{"tool": "read", "sources": ["USPSTF colorectal cancer screening recommendation 2021", "NCCN colorectal cancer genetic/familial high-risk assessment updates", "recent EOCRC clinical reviews 2022-2024"]}
```

[result]: Core findings: average-risk screening now begins at 45 in the U.S.; symptomatic evaluation remains critical because many EOCRC cases occur before screening eligibility. All CRC tumors should undergo mismatch-repair/MSI testing, and germline testing is strongly indicated for CRC diagnosed before 50, with increasing use of broad multigene panels. Treatment generally follows stage- and biomarker-based principles used in older adults, but EOCRC care must address fertility, sexual function, neuropathy risk, ostomy concerns, psychosocial burden, and long-term survivorship.

{"tool": "propose", "answer": "Recent advances in early-onset colorectal cancer (EOCRC), usually defined as CRC diagnosed before age 50, center on earlier detection, broader genetic evaluation, and more individualized management. Rising incidence—particularly of distal colon and rectal cancers—has shifted clinical emphasis from viewing CRC as primarily a disease of older adults to maintaining suspicion in younger patients with rectal bleeding, iron-deficiency anemia, change in bowel habits, unexplained abdominal pain, or weight loss. A major diagnostic priority is reducing delays caused by attributing symptoms to benign conditions such as hemorrhoids or irritable bowel syndrome.\n\nScreening guidance has changed substantially. In the United States, average-risk colorectal cancer screening is now generally recommended to begin at age 45 rather than 50, using colonoscopy or stool-based options such as annual FIT, stool DNA-FIT, or other guideline-endorsed strategies. People with family history, known hereditary syndromes, inflammatory bowel disease, prior advanced polyps, or abdominal/pelvic radiation may need earlier and more intensive screening. However, because many EOCRC cases occur before age 45, screening alone is insufficient; timely diagnostic colonoscopy for concerning symptoms remains essential.\n\nGenetic evaluation has also expanded. Universal tumor testing for mismatch repair deficiency or microsatellite instability is standard and helps identify Lynch syndrome and guide treatment, especially immunotherapy decisions in advanced disease. For patients diagnosed before age 50, germline evaluation with multigene panels is increasingly recommended because pathogenic variants may be found beyond classic Lynch-associated genes. Results affect patient management, surveillance for metachronous cancers, surgical planning in selected cases, and cascade testing for relatives.\n\nTreatment considerations generally follow standard stage- and biomarker-based colorectal cancer care, including surgery, chemotherapy, radiation for selected rectal cancers, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy for MSI-high/dMMR tumors. Recent advances include total neoadjuvant therapy for many locally advanced rectal cancers, organ-preservation or watch-and-wait strategies in carefully selected complete responders, and biomarker-driven systemic therapy in metastatic disease. In EOCRC specifically, management should integrate fertility preservation, pregnancy considerations, sexual and bowel function, neuropathy and long-term toxicity, ostomy counseling, financial toxicity, mental health, and survivorship planning, since younger patients often face decades of post-treatment consequences."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/materials] Propose research directions for low-carbon cementitious materials that maintain structural performance while reducing clinker content, incorporating supplementa
full_prompt: Propose research directions for low-carbon cementitious materials that maintain structural performance while reducing clinker content, incorporating supplementary cementitious materials, carbon curing, or alternative binders.
completion:
Rationale: First I scan recent review/benchmark literature to identify mature low-clinker options, performance constraints, and gaps where a short proposal can be both plausible and novel.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"low clinker cement LC3 supplementary cementitious materials carbon curing alternative binders structural performance review 2018 2024","discipline":"materials","limit":5}
```
[result]: Simulated results: (1) Scrivener, Martirena, Bishnoi & Maity, “Calcined clay limestone cements (LC3),” Cement and Concrete Research 2018, DOI 10.1016/j.cemconres.2017.08.017, OpenAlex candidate: lookup by DOI available. (2) Zhang et al., “Carbonation curing of cement-based materials: mechanisms and performance,” Construction and Building Materials/Cem. Concr. Compos. review cluster, 2020–2023. (3) Juenger et al., “Supplementary cementitious materials for concrete: characterization needs,” Cement and Concrete Research, DOI-linked OpenAlex candidate. (4) Provis, “Alkali-activated materials,” Cement and Concrete Research 2018, DOI-linked OpenAlex candidate. (5) Recent papers show gaps in combined LC3/carbon curing, durability-linked optimization, and SCM variability management.

Rationale: I check whether a direction combining SCM chemistry, CO₂ curing, and structural validation is likely incremental or distinctive.
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"performance-based design of low-clinker concretes combining calcined clay/limestone/industrial SCM blends with controlled early-age CO2 curing and data-driven mixture adjustment, validated at member scale for durability and embodied carbon","candidates":["DOI:10.1016/j.cemconres.2017.08.017","DOI:10.1016/j.cemconres.2017.02.009","DOI-linked OpenAlex candidates for carbonation curing reviews and alkali-activated materials reviews"]}
```
[result]: Simulated novelty assessment: High applied novelty. Individual components are well studied, but few programs integrate variable SCM feedstocks, carbonation kinetics, pore/refinement chemistry, reinforced-member performance, and life-cycle/structural reliability constraints into one design framework. Best angle: hybrid low-clinker binders where carbon curing is used not only for CO₂ uptake but also to compensate early-strength and durability penalties from clinker dilution.
{"tool":"propose","answer":"Develop a performance-based platform for low-carbon structural concretes that combines reduced-clinker Portland cement with calcined clay–limestone systems, locally available SCMs such as slag, fly ash, natural pozzolans, or recycled fines, and controlled early-age CO₂ curing. The central hypothesis is that carbonation curing can be tuned to densify pore structure and accelerate early strength, offsetting the slower hydration or variable reactivity of high-SCM binders while permanently mineralizing a small but measurable amount of CO₂.\n\nA strong research direction is to build mixture–process–performance maps rather than test single “green” recipes. Use thermodynamic modeling, calorimetry, XRD/TGA, MIP or sorption methods, and nano/micro-mechanical testing to link SCM chemistry, clinker replacement level, carbonation exposure, moisture state, and curing duration to strength development, shrinkage, permeability, chloride ingress, sulfate resistance, and reinforcement corrosion risk. This should include realistic SCM variability, because future low-carbon cement will rely on heterogeneous regional feedstocks rather than uniform fly ash or slag supplies.\n\nThe most valuable experimental program would validate optimized binders in structural elements, not only paste or mortar. Candidate systems could include 40–60% clinker replacement LC3-type binders, ternary blends with recycled concrete fines, and hybrid alkali-activated/Portland binders. Beams, slabs, or columns should be tested for compressive strength, modulus, bond strength, cracking, creep, and flexural capacity after conventional curing versus CO₂-assisted curing, with durability exposure continued for months.\n\nThe outcome should be a design framework that reports embodied carbon per unit structural performance, such as kg CO₂e per MPa strength or per kN·m flexural capacity, while accounting for CO₂ uptake during curing. This would help move low-clinker materials from material substitution toward reliability-based structural design, enabling standards-ready recipes that reduce clinker content without sacrificing safety, durability, or constructability."}

OpenAlex IDs were not resolved in this simulated pass, so DOI-backed candidates were retained.

Rationale: check whether the proposed comparison can contribute beyond standard reviews by emphasizing a shared mechanism-versus-scalability framework.
```action novelty_check
{"claim": "A comparative framework for lightweight impact-resistant structures that contrasts nacre-like composites, graphene-reinforced polymers, and ceramic matrix composites by toughness mechanism, rate sensitivity, damage tolerance, and scalable processing route."}
```
[result]: Novelty appears moderate-to-high as a synthesis proposal rather than a new material claim. Existing reviews usually treat bioinspired architectures, graphene-polymer nanocomposites, or CMCs separately. The gap is a cross-family design map linking mechanisms such as crack deflection, platelet pullout, interfacial sliding, nanofiller bridging, fiber/matrix debonding, and oxidation/thermal limits to practical scale-up constraints.

{"tool": "propose", "answer": "I propose a short comparative study of three leading classes of lightweight impact-resistant materials: bioinspired nacre-like composites, graphene-reinforced polymers, and ceramic matrix composites. The central question is not simply which material is toughest, but which toughening mechanisms remain effective when translated from laboratory coupons to manufacturable structural components.\n\nThe study will compare mechanisms across scales. Nacre-like composites derive toughness from brick-and-mortar architectures, crack deflection, platelet sliding, mineral bridge fracture, and polymeric energy dissipation; their advantage is graceful damage tolerance, but alignment and large-area processing remain limiting. Graphene-reinforced polymers use high-aspect-ratio nanofillers for crack bridging, load transfer, interfacial friction, and local plastic-zone modification; they are attractive for low-density panels, but dispersion, agglomeration, interfacial control, and reproducibility often limit gains at scale. Ceramic matrix composites rely on fiber pullout, matrix microcracking, crack deflection, and engineered fiber/matrix debonding, giving excellent high-temperature and erosion resistance, though at high cost and with complex infiltration or sintering routes.\n\nThe proposed outcome is a mechanism–scalability map for impact-resistant structures. For each class, I would score density-normalized toughness, impact energy absorption, thermal capability, environmental durability, processing maturity, repairability, and cost. This comparison should clarify that nacre-like composites are promising for bioinspired armor and protective panels, graphene polymers for scalable lightweight skins and secondary structures, and CMCs for extreme thermal or aerospace environments where cost is justified.\n\nThe final deliverable would be a concise design guide identifying hybrid opportunities, such as graphene-toughened nacre-like polymer matrices or hierarchical CMCs with nacre-inspired interfaces. This would help materials researchers move beyond isolated performance claims toward architecture-specific recommendations for real lightweight impact-resistant systems."}

full_prompt: Analyze evidence-based strategies to reduce hospital readmissions among older adults with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, including discharge planning, pharmacologic therapy, pulmonary rehabilitation, and follow-up care.
completion:

```action

```
[result]: Found candidate sources: GOLD 2024 COPD report; ATS/ERS COPD exacerbation and pulmonary rehabilitation guidance; Cochrane reviews on discharge planning and COPD self-management; RCTs/cohorts on hospital-to-home bundles and early post-discharge follow-up. OpenAlex candidates include W2741809807 (GOLD-related COPD strategy report records), W2108511765 (pulmonary rehabilitation after exacerbation systematic review), and W2046920530 (self-management interventions in COPD).
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project will synthesize evidence-based strategies to reduce 30- and 90-day hospital readmissions among older adults hospitalized for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The review will focus on four intervention domains: structured discharge planning, optimized pharmacologic therapy, pulmonary rehabilitation, and post-discharge follow-up. Because older adults often have multimorbidity, frailty, polypharmacy, and social barriers, the analysis will emphasize interventions that are feasible in real-world geriatric and transitional-care settings.

The discharge-planning component will examine COPD-specific bundles that include inhaler technique assessment, medication reconciliation, written action plans, smoking cessation support, vaccination review, oxygen reassessment, and identification of comorbid triggers such as heart failure or infection. Pharmacologic therapy will be evaluated through evidence on appropriate long-acting bronchodilator use, escalation to dual bronchodilation or inhaled corticosteroid-containing regimens when indicated, avoidance of unnecessary corticosteroid exposure, and short-course treatment of acute exacerbations. Particular attention will be paid to safety issues in older adults, including pneumonia risk, osteoporosis, arrhythmia, and medication adherence.

The pulmonary rehabilitation section will assess evidence for early post-exacerbation rehabilitation, home-based or tele-rehabilitation alternatives, and barriers to uptake after discharge. Follow-up care will be analyzed across timing and modality, including primary care or pulmonology review within 7–30 days, nurse-led transitional care, telephone monitoring, home visits, remote symptom tracking, and rapid access pathways for recurrent exacerbation symptoms. Outcomes will include readmission, mortality, emergency visits, health-related quality of life, dyspnea, functional status, and patient burden.

The final product will be a concise evidence map and clinical recommendation framework identifying which interventions have the strongest support, which are most applicable to older adults, and where evidence remains uncertain. The proposal will prioritize bundled, patient-centered transitional care rather than isolated interventions, with the hypothesis that readmission reduction is most likely when discharge planning, medication optimization, rehabilitation referral, and timely follow-up are coordinated across hospital and community care."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/clinical_medicine] Evaluate best practices for diagnosing and treating long COVID in primary care, including common symptom clusters, recommended workup, and multidisciplinary man
full_prompt: Evaluate best practices for diagnosing and treating long COVID in primary care, including common symptom clusters, recommended workup, and multidisciplinary management.
completion:
Rationale: I first scoped authoritative clinical sources and recent syntheses to anchor the primary-care recommendations in guidelines rather than isolated studies.
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "long COVID primary care diagnosis management guideline symptom clusters workup multidisciplinary rehabilitation NICE CDC WHO BMJ", "discipline": "clinical_medicine", "length_tier": "short"}
```
[result]: Candidates identified included NICE/SIGN/RCGP guidance on managing long-term effects of COVID-19, CDC clinical guidance for post-COVID conditions, WHO clinical case definition and rehabilitation guidance, and primary-care reviews in BMJ and Lancet Respiratory Medicine. Commonly recurring themes were symptom-cluster recognition, exclusion of alternative diagnoses, safety screening for red flags, pacing and rehabilitation, dysautonomia assessment, mental-health support, and referral pathways.

Rationale: I then extracted the convergent clinical recommendations most relevant to family medicine visits: diagnostic criteria, initial investigations, and care coordination.
```action
{"tool": "read", "sources": ["NICE NG188 long-term effects of COVID-19", "CDC Post-COVID Conditions clinical guidance", "WHO post COVID-19 condition clinical case definition", "BMJ primary care long COVID management review"]}
```
[result]: Across sources, long COVID is usually considered when symptoms persist or recur beyond 4–12 weeks after probable or confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection and are not better explained by another diagnosis. Frequent clusters include fatigue and post-exertional symptom exacerbation, dyspnea and cough, chest pain and palpitations, cognitive dysfunction or “brain fog,” sleep disturbance, anxiety/depression, headache, anosmia/dysgeusia, gastrointestinal symptoms, myalgia/arthralgia, and autonomic symptoms such as orthostatic intolerance. Recommended initial workup is symptom-led and may include vitals including orthostatic measurements, oxygen saturation and exertional desaturation testing, CBC, CMP, CRP/ESR, thyroid function, HbA1c, ferritin/iron studies, B12/folate when indicated, ECG, chest imaging or pulmonary function testing for cardiopulmonary symptoms, and targeted evaluation for thromboembolism, myocarditis, heart failure, endocrine disease, anemia, sleep apnea, or mental-health disorders. Management emphasizes validation, shared decision-making, self-management education, pacing, treatment of comorbidities, rehabilitation tailored to post-exertional malaise, and multidisciplinary referral when symptoms are severe, progressive, or function-limiting.

{"tool": "propose", "answer": "I propose a concise primary-care framework for diagnosing and treating long COVID that begins with recognition: patients may present with persistent, relapsing, or new symptoms after suspected or confirmed COVID-19, typically beyond 4 weeks and especially beyond 12 weeks, without an alternative explanation. The most common symptom clusters to screen for are fatigue with post-exertional symptom exacerbation, respiratory symptoms such as dyspnea or cough, cardiovascular symptoms such as chest pain or palpitations, cognitive and neurologic symptoms including brain fog and headache, sleep disturbance, mood symptoms, pain syndromes, gastrointestinal complaints, and autonomic symptoms such as dizziness or orthostatic intolerance.\n\nThe recommended workup in primary care should be symptom-directed rather than indiscriminate. A baseline visit should document pre-COVID function, timing of infection, vaccination and reinfection history, medication use, comorbidities, occupational and social impact, and red flags. Examination should include vital signs, pulse oximetry, and orthostatic measurements when dizziness, palpitations, or presyncope are present. Common initial tests include CBC, metabolic panel, inflammatory markers when clinically indicated, thyroid function, HbA1c, ferritin or iron studies, B12/folate if neuropathy or cognitive symptoms are prominent, ECG for chest pain or palpitations, and chest imaging or spirometry for persistent respiratory symptoms. Clinicians should actively exclude serious mimics such as pulmonary embolism, myocarditis, heart failure, uncontrolled asthma or COPD, anemia, endocrine disease, sleep apnea, autoimmune disease, medication adverse effects, depression, and anxiety.\n\nTreatment should focus on validation, functional recovery, and prevention of harm. Patients with post-exertional symptom exacerbation should be advised to use pacing and energy conservation rather than fixed graded exercise escalation. Breathlessness may benefit from breathing retraining, pulmonary rehabilitation when appropriate, and treatment of underlying airway disease. Dysautonomia symptoms can be managed initially with hydration, salt intake when safe, compression garments, and recumbent or carefully titrated activity, with referral if severe. Cognitive symptoms may require sleep optimization, workload modification, cognitive pacing, and occupational therapy. Mood symptoms should be treated with standard evidence-based psychological and pharmacologic care while avoiding dismissal of physical symptoms as purely psychological.\n\nBest practice is multidisciplinary, stepped, and longitudinal. Primary care should coordinate referrals to cardiology, pulmonology, neurology, rehabilitation medicine, physical or occupational therapy, mental health, social work, and specialized long-COVID clinics according to symptom burden and red flags. Follow-up should track patient-defined goals, activities of daily living, work or school participation, symptom flares, and quality of life. The overall care model should be equitable and pragmatic: confirm the syndrome clinically, rule out dangerous alternatives, treat specific symptoms and comorbidities, support disability accommodations when needed, and maintain continuity because recovery trajectories are variable."}

full_prompt: Analyze how sustained high interest rates affect small business lending, bankruptcy risk, and employment in developed economies. Respond only as free_text.
completion:
I’ll first map the closest empirical literatures linking monetary tightening to small-firm credit constraints, distress, and labor demand.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"monetary policy tightening small business lending credit constraints bankruptcy employment developed economies","candidates":[{"title":"Monetary Policy and Bank Lending","authors":"Kashyap and Stein","venue":"NBER / monetary transmission literature","openalex_id":"available in OpenAlex but not resolved in simulated run"},{"title":"The Credit Channel of Monetary Policy Transmission","authors":"Bernanke and Gertler","venue":"Journal of Economic Perspectives","openalex_id":"available in OpenAlex but not resolved in simulated run"},{"title":"Small Business Lending in Financial Crises and Monetary Tightening","authors":"various OECD and central-bank studies","venue":"policy and empirical finance literature","openalex_id":"not applicable"}]}
```

[result]: The core mechanism is the bank-lending and balance-sheet channel: sustained high policy rates raise banks’ funding costs and risk premia, reduce borrower net worth, and disproportionately restrict credit to opaque, collateral-constrained small firms. Evidence is strongest where small firms rely heavily on relationship banks rather than bond markets.

Next I’ll check what would be novel relative to standard monetary-transmission and SME-finance work.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"A comparative developed-economy study linking the duration of high-rate episodes to SME loan quantities, bankruptcy filings, and employment adjustment using bank exposure and firm-level heterogeneity.","against":"monetary transmission, SME credit constraints, bankruptcy prediction, labor demand under financial frictions"}
```

[result]: The novelty lies less in showing that higher rates reduce credit, and more in jointly estimating the dynamic sequence from lending contraction to insolvency risk to employment losses across developed economies, distinguishing short rate hikes from sustained high-rate regimes and separating demand for credit from bank supply.

{"tool": "propose", "answer": "This project would analyze how sustained high interest rates affect small business lending, bankruptcy risk, and employment in developed economies. The central hypothesis is that prolonged high-rate environments transmit to small firms through both a bank-lending channel and a balance-sheet channel: banks tighten credit as funding costs and default risk rise, while small firms face weaker cash flow, lower collateral values, and higher debt-service burdens. Because small businesses typically have limited access to capital markets, these effects should be stronger than for large firms.\n\nEmpirically, the study would build a panel of developed economies, combining central-bank policy rates, SME loan volumes and interest spreads, bankruptcy or insolvency filings, and small-firm employment measures. The main design would compare sectors, regions, or banks with different pre-existing exposure to floating-rate debt, deposit outflows, or SME lending. This would help distinguish a true credit-supply contraction from weaker loan demand caused by slower aggregate activity.\n\nThe expected finding is a staggered effect: lending conditions deteriorate first through higher spreads, shorter maturities, and tighter collateral requirements; bankruptcy risk rises with a lag as refinancing becomes difficult; and employment falls through reduced hiring, layoffs, or firm exits. The effects should be largest among young firms, highly leveraged firms, firms in interest-sensitive sectors, and economies where SME finance is bank-dependent rather than market-based.\n\nThe contribution would be to connect three outcomes—credit, insolvency, and employment—within one framework for modern developed economies. This would be useful for evaluating whether monetary tightening has disproportionate real effects on small businesses and whether targeted credit guarantees, restructuring tools, or liquidity facilities can reduce unnecessary bankruptcies without undermining the inflation-fighting role of higher rates."}

[result]: Simulated search result: the field is dominated by 1.65–1.80 eV mixed-halide Pb perovskites for top cells on Si and 1.2–1.3 eV Sn–Pb low-bandgap perovskites for all-perovskite tandems. Key recurring bottlenecks are photoinduced halide segregation, interfacial recombination, thermal/moisture/oxygen instability, Sn oxidation, lead toxicity/end-of-life control, and coating uniformity over textured or large-area substrates.

Next I’ll read representative papers/reviews to identify the current consensus on absorber design rules and where the literature still disagrees.  
```action
{"tool":"read","ids":["10.1126/science.aba3433","10.1038/s41560-021-00894-9","10.1038/s41578-020-0189-2","10.1038/s41560-020-00710-8","10.1038/s41560-022-01012-1"],"focus":["absorber bandgap targets","ion migration and phase segregation","lead mitigation","scalable deposition","performance ceilings"]}
```  
[result]: Simulated reading result: high-performance Si tandems require wide-bandgap absorbers near ~1.68 eV but suffer voltage deficits from bulk/interfacial defects and halide segregation. All-perovskite tandems promise lightweight/flexible modules but rely on metastable Sn–Pb absorbers whose Sn2+ oxidation and high defect density limit lifetime and voltage. Stability protocols increasingly distinguish operational maximum-power tracking, damp heat, thermal cycling, UV exposure, and reverse-bias/shading tolerance. Scaling from spin-coated lab cells to blade/slot-die/vapor-assisted films introduces new failure modes: wetting, drying-front gradients, grain-size inhomogeneity, and substrate-texture conformality.

I’ll extract the “materials genome” of tandem absorbers: composition, instability mode, mitigation strategy, and remaining limitation.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","corpus":"tandem_perovskite_absorber_literature_2020_2024","fields":["absorber_family","typical_bandgap","tandem_use","key_additives_or_composition","main_stability_risk","lead_or_tin_issue","scalable_deposition_status","performance_limit"]}
```  
[result]: Simulated extraction result:  
- Mixed I/Br Pb perovskites, ~1.65–1.80 eV, Si top cell: stabilized by Cs/FA mixtures, pseudo-halides, 2D/3D passivation, alkali salts, and interface dipoles; main risks are Br/I segregation, ion migration, UV/thermal degradation, and nonradiative recombination.  
- Sn–Pb perovskites, ~1.20–1.35 eV, all-perovskite bottom cell: stabilized by SnF2, reducing additives, bulky cations, antioxidant processing, and oxygen exclusion; main risks are Sn vacancies, Sn2+ oxidation, self-doping, and rapid burn-in.  
- Lead-reduced/lead-free options such as Sn-only, Ge-, Bi-, Sb-, double perovskites: environmentally attractive but presently too voltage- and mobility-limited for leading tandems.  
- Deposition: spin coating remains best for record cells; blade coating, slot-die coating, evaporation/co-evaporation, hybrid vapor–solution routes, and inkjet/spray are maturing but lag in reproducibility, defect control, and textured-wafer coverage.

I’ll check what would be novel and useful as a research direction rather than merely summarizing current reviews.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"A research program that couples compositionally graded mixed-halide wide-bandgap perovskites with lead-sequestering module architectures and scalable slot-die/vapor-assisted deposition, using linked operando photoluminescence, ion-migration mapping, and accelerated tandem-relevant stress tests to define absorber design rules.","benchmarks":["wide-bandgap perovskite passivation studies","perovskite/silicon tandem commercialization reviews","lead sequestration and encapsulation studies","slot-die coated perovskite module reports","Sn-Pb all-perovskite tandem studies"]}
```  
[result]: Simulated novelty check: partially novel. Individual elements exist, but the integrated absorber-centric framework—composition gradient + scalable deposition + embedded lead capture + tandem-specific stability/performance-limit metrology—is underdeveloped. Strongest contribution would be a comparative, mechanism-driven roadmap linking absorber chemistry to manufacturable tandem module reliability.

Now I’ll synthesize this into a concise research proposal that discusses stability, lead mitigation, scalable deposition, and performance limits.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project will analyze and advance perovskite absorber materials for tandem photovoltaics by focusing on the two compositions that currently define the field: wide-bandgap mixed-halide Pb perovskites for perovskite/silicon tandems and low-bandgap Sn–Pb perovskites for all-perovskite tandems. The central hypothesis is that tandem performance is now limited less by single-junction optical absorption and more by absorber metastability, ion migration, interfacial voltage loss, and the difficulty of reproducing record-cell microstructures in scalable manufacturing. A systematic comparison of FA/Cs mixed I/Br absorbers, compositionally graded wide-bandgap films, and Sn–Pb low-bandgap alloys would identify which chemistries can realistically support stable modules rather than only record small-area cells.\n\nThe stability work package should combine tandem-relevant stress testing with operando diagnostics. For wide-bandgap absorbers, the priority is suppressing photoinduced halide segregation, Br-rich/I-rich phase formation, and bias-driven ion accumulation at charge-selective contacts. For Sn–Pb absorbers, the key targets are Sn2+ oxidation, Sn-vacancy self-doping, and rapid burn-in under heat and illumination. The proposal would use operando photoluminescence mapping, electroluminescence, time-resolved spectroscopy, and ion-migration-sensitive impedance or Kelvin-probe methods during maximum-power-point operation, damp heat, thermal cycling, UV exposure, and partial-shading/reverse-bias tests.\n\nLead mitigation should be treated as a module-level materials problem, not only a chemistry substitution problem. Near-term tandems will likely remain Pb-based because Pb-free and Pb-reduced absorbers still suffer large voltage deficits, poor transport, or inferior stability. Therefore, the program should evaluate lead-sequestering encapsulants, ion-exchange layers, edge seals, and recyclable module stacks alongside absorber design. For all-perovskite tandems, parallel study of Sn-rich and Sn–Pb absorbers should quantify whether reduced lead content compensates for the greater instability and potential manufacturing yield losses caused by tin oxidation.\n\nScalable deposition is the decisive filter for absorber relevance. The project should benchmark spin-coated reference films against slot-die coating, blade coating, vapor-assisted conversion, and co-evaporation on planar glass, textured silicon, and flexible substrates. The key metrics should include bandgap uniformity, residual strain, defect density, vertical halide gradients, film conformality on textured wafers, and cell-to-module efficiency loss. A useful outcome would be processing maps that connect ink rheology, drying kinetics, antisolvent-free crystallization, and post-treatment chemistry to absorber uniformity over large areas.\n\nThe expected result is a practical performance-limit roadmap for tandem absorbers. For perovskite/silicon tandems, the main ceiling is the voltage and stability penalty of ~1.7 eV mixed-halide top cells, especially under real outdoor spectra and temperature cycling. For all-perovskite tandems, the ceiling is set by the combined instability of wide-bandgap mixed-halide top cells and Sn–Pb bottom cells. By linking absorber chemistry, scalable deposition, lead containment, and operational degradation mechanisms, this research would clarify which materials pathways can move tandem perovskites from record efficiencies toward bankable photovoltaic modules."}
```  
[result]: Simulated propose result: accepted.
