topic: [en/free_text_answer/materials] Review recent advances in high-entropy alloys for high-temperature turbine applications, including composition design strategies, oxidation resistance, creep be
full_prompt: Review recent advances in high-entropy alloys for high-temperature turbine applications, including composition design strategies, oxidation resistance, creep behavior, and barriers to commercialization.
completion:
I’ll first identify recent review and perspective articles that anchor the state of the field, especially those connecting high-entropy alloys to gas-turbine operating requirements rather than only general HEA science.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"recent review high entropy alloys high temperature turbine applications oxidation creep composition design","candidates":[{"title":"High-entropy alloys for high-temperature applications","doi":"10.1038/s41578-019-0121-4","openalex_id":"W2963071407"},{"title":"High-entropy alloys: A critical review","doi":"10.1016/j.mser.2018.04.001","openalex_id":"W2807702211"},{"title":"A review of high-entropy alloys and their mechanical properties","doi":"10.1016/j.mser.2014.04.001","openalex_id":"W2031478637"}]}
```

[result]: The literature clusters into three relevant streams: refractory HEAs for extreme-temperature strength, γ/γ′-strengthened compositionally complex alloys inspired by Ni superalloys, and environmental-damage studies focused on oxidation/hot corrosion. The turbine-relevant reviews emphasize that strength alone is insufficient; density, oxidation resistance, phase stability, creep, manufacturability, and cost must be assessed together.

Next I’ll read the turbine-adjacent sources to extract the main composition-design strategies and how they differ from conventional Ni-base superalloy design.

```action
{"tool":"read","ids":["W2963071407","W2807702211"],"extract":["composition_design","turbine_relevance","limitations"]}
```

[result]: Key design strategies include: 1) refractory BCC/B2 alloys based on Nb-Mo-Ta-W-V-Ti-Zr-Hf for high melting point and sluggish diffusion, 2) FCC CoCrFeNi-derived systems modified with Al, Ti, Nb, Mo, or Ta for precipitation strengthening, 3) γ/γ′ high-entropy or medium-entropy superalloys using Ni-Co-Fe-Cr-Al-Ti/Nb/Ta chemistries, and 4) CALPHAD/machine-learning screening to avoid brittle intermetallics and topologically close-packed phases. The main limitation is that many refractory HEAs oxidize poorly, while many oxidation-resistant FCC alloys lack creep strength above ~800–900 °C.

I’ll now focus on oxidation resistance, because it is one of the most important barriers for turbine hot-section use and often invalidates otherwise promising refractory compositions.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"oxidation resistance refractory high entropy alloys Al Cr Si turbine high temperature review","candidates":[{"title":"Oxidation behavior of high-entropy alloys: A review","doi":"10.1016/j.corsci.2020.108962","openalex_id":"W3034129102"},{"title":"High temperature oxidation behavior of refractory high entropy alloys","doi":"10.1016/j.corsci.2018.10.007","openalex_id":"W2895434369"},{"title":"Oxidation behavior of AlxCoCrFeNi high entropy alloys","doi":"10.1016/j.corsci.2013.08.030","openalex_id":"W2012677442"}]}
```

[result]: Oxidation-resistant HEA design commonly aims to form continuous Al₂O₃ or Cr₂O₃ scales, often by adding Al, Cr, and sometimes Si. However, refractory HEAs containing Nb, Ta, Ti, Zr, Hf, or Mo often form porous, nonprotective mixed oxides. Al additions can improve oxidation but may promote B2 ordering, embrittlement, or phase instability. Coatings may be necessary for many refractory HEAs, reducing the advantage of intrinsic alloy design.

I’ll extract the “genome” of candidate alloy families: element sets, strengthening mechanisms, target temperature ranges, oxidation mechanisms, and likely turbine relevance.

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","corpus":"recent_HEA_turbine_literature","fields":["alloy_family","representative_elements","matrix_phase","strengthening","oxidation_strategy","creep_status","density_concern","turbine_readiness"]}
```

[result]: Four alloy genomes emerge. Family A: refractory BCC HEAs such as NbMoTaW, VNbMoTaW, HfNbTaTiZr, and Al-containing derivatives; strengths are high above 1000 °C, but density and oxidation are serious problems. Family B: lightweight refractory HEAs using Ti-Zr-Nb-V-Al or Ti-Al-Cr-Nb systems; density is improved, but oxidation and creep databases remain sparse. Family C: FCC CoCrFeNi-based alloys; corrosion and ductility are good, but high-temperature strength is usually inadequate unless strengthened by precipitates or L1₂ phases. Family D: γ/γ′ high-entropy or medium-entropy superalloys based on Ni-Co-Fe-Cr-Al-Ti-Ta/Nb; these are closest to turbine practice because they mirror Ni-superalloy strengthening and oxidation concepts.

I’ll examine creep behavior specifically, since turbine blades and vanes are creep-limited components and short-term compression strength can be misleading.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"creep behavior high entropy alloys high temperature refractory HEA gamma prime turbine","candidates":[{"title":"Creep behavior of a refractory high entropy alloy at elevated temperatures","doi":"10.1016/j.scriptamat.2016.07.003","openalex_id":"W2508713764"},{"title":"Exceptional high-temperature strength in refractory high-entropy alloys","doi":"10.1016/j.scriptamat.2015.06.028","openalex_id":"W2333016415"},{"title":"A precipitation-hardened high-entropy alloy with outstanding tensile properties","doi":"10.1038/nature22811","openalex_id":"W2628530191"}]}
```

[result]: Creep evidence is much thinner than yield-strength data. Refractory HEAs can show high strength retention but often suffer from limited tensile ductility, oxidation during long tests, and unstable microstructures. Precipitation-strengthened FCC/L1₂ HEAs show a clearer path to creep resistance because coherent precipitates, solid-solution strengthening, and grain-boundary engineering can be adapted from Ni-base superalloys. However, few studies yet match industrial creep-test durations, multiaxial stresses, thermal cycling, or coated-environment conditions.

I’ll compare the HEA design space against mature Ni-base turbine superalloys to clarify where HEAs may offer real advantages and where they currently fall short.

```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","baseline":"Ni-base single-crystal and polycrystalline superalloys","comparison":"high-entropy and compositionally complex alloys","dimensions":["melting_temperature","density","creep_strength","oxidation","phase_stability","castability","coating_compatibility","cost"]}
```

[result]: HEAs may offer broader compositional flexibility, high configurational/chemical complexity, strong solid-solution hardening, and access to refractory-rich matrices beyond conventional Ni alloys. Ni-base superalloys still dominate in γ/γ′ stability, process maturity, oxidation/coating compatibility, creep databases, and repairability. Refractory HEAs can exceed superalloys in melting point and short-term strength but are often too dense or oxidation-prone. γ/γ′ compositionally complex superalloys are the most commercially plausible HEA-derived path because they extend, rather than replace, existing turbine metallurgy.

I’ll run a novelty and gap check to define the best contribution: not another generic HEA review, but a turbine-specific synthesis organized around certification barriers.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"A turbine-focused review of HEAs integrating composition design, oxidation, creep, manufacturability, and commercialization barriers","against":["W2963071407","W2807702211","W3034129102","W2508713764","W2628530191"]}
```

[result]: Novelty is moderate to high if the review is framed around turbine qualification rather than alloy discovery. Existing reviews cover HEAs broadly, refractory alloys, oxidation, or mechanical properties, but fewer integrate all turbine-readiness constraints: creep life, cyclic oxidation, thermal-barrier-coating compatibility, density-normalized properties, casting/additive manufacturing, defect tolerance, raw-material supply, and certification datasets.

{"tool":"propose","answer":"This review will synthesize recent advances in high-entropy alloys and compositionally complex alloys for high-temperature turbine applications, with emphasis on the gap between promising laboratory properties and turbine-component qualification. The article should begin by distinguishing four major alloy-design routes: refractory BCC/B2 HEAs for ultra-high-temperature strength, lightweight refractory variants, FCC CoCrFeNi-derived alloys, and γ/γ′ high- or medium-entropy superalloys that borrow directly from Ni-base superalloy metallurgy. This framing will help show that the most turbine-relevant progress is not simply the use of many principal elements, but the deliberate coupling of phase stability, precipitation strengthening, oxidation resistance, and density-normalized performance.\n\nA central section should evaluate composition design strategies. CALPHAD, high-throughput experiments, and machine-learning approaches are increasingly used to screen large composition spaces, suppress brittle intermetallics, and stabilize useful FCC/L1₂ or BCC/B2 microstructures. Refractory systems based on Nb, Mo, Ta, W, Ti, Zr, Hf, and V offer high melting points and excellent short-term strength, but their density, room-temperature ductility, and environmental resistance remain major concerns. By contrast, Ni-Co-Fe-Cr-Al-Ti-Ta/Nb γ/γ′ compositionally complex superalloys are less radical but more commercially plausible because they align with known turbine strengthening mechanisms and processing routes.\n\nThe review should then treat oxidation resistance and creep behavior as decisive screening criteria. Oxidation-resistant HEAs generally require continuous Al₂O₃ or Cr₂O₃ scales, yet many refractory HEAs form porous mixed oxides containing Nb, Ti, Ta, Mo, Zr, or Hf and therefore require alloy modification or coatings. Creep data are also much less mature than compression or tensile-strength data. Promising HEAs must be compared using long-duration, environment-controlled creep tests, thermal cycling, microstructural stability studies, and density-normalized metrics rather than short-term strength alone.\n\nFinally, the review should identify commercialization barriers: limited property databases, uncertain castability and segregation control, coating compatibility, weldability and repair, raw-material cost and supply risk, scale-up reproducibility, and certification under turbine-relevant duty cycles. The likely near-term impact of HEA research is not the wholesale replacement of Ni-base superalloys, but the discovery of compositionally complex superalloys and bond-coat or environmental-resistant systems that extend existing turbine materials platforms. The proposed contribution is therefore a turbine-readiness map that ranks alloy families by strength, oxidation resistance, creep evidence, process maturity, and qualification risk."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/economics_finance] Investigate how housing supply constraints influence wealth inequality across generations in major metropolitan areas. Respond in free-text format without table
full_prompt: Investigate how housing supply constraints influence wealth inequality across generations in major metropolitan areas. Respond in free-text format without tables or code.
completion:
Rationale: I would first map the core empirical literatures linking housing supply elasticity, price capitalization, and wealth accumulation, prioritizing papers with metropolitan variation and intergenerational implications.
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "housing supply constraints wealth inequality generations metropolitan areas economics"}
```
[result]: Key candidate literatures identified: urban housing supply elasticity and land-use regulation; housing wealth and intergenerational transfers; spatial sorting and access to high-opportunity metros; and life-cycle wealth accumulation through owner-occupied housing. Representative works include Saiz (2010), “The Geographic Determinants of Housing Supply”; Gyourko, Mayer, and Sinai (2013), “Superstar Cities”; Hilber and Vermeulen (2016), “The Impact of Supply Constraints on House Prices in England”; and studies on parental housing wealth, down-payment assistance, and young-adult homeownership.

Rationale: Next I would compare the “genome” of this topic against nearby literatures to isolate the novel contribution: not just whether constraints raise prices, but whether they reshape the intergenerational distribution of housing wealth within and across major metros.
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "candidates": ["housing supply elasticity and house prices", "zoning regulation and inequality", "intergenerational housing wealth transfers", "metropolitan housing affordability and wealth gaps"]}
```
[result]: Novel opportunity: existing research often treats supply constraints, affordability, and wealth inequality separately. A promising contribution is to estimate how constrained supply amplifies wealth divergence between older incumbent homeowners and younger renter or first-time-buyer cohorts, and how this varies across high-demand metropolitan areas.

{"tool": "propose", "answer": "This project would investigate how housing supply constraints shape wealth inequality across generations in major metropolitan areas. The central hypothesis is that restrictive land-use regimes and inelastic housing supply convert metropolitan growth into capital gains for incumbent homeowners, while raising entry costs for younger households. As a result, constrained metros may produce a dual intergenerational effect: older owners accumulate housing wealth through scarcity-driven appreciation, while younger cohorts face delayed homeownership, higher rent burdens, and greater dependence on family transfers.\n\nEmpirically, the study would combine metropolitan measures of housing supply elasticity, land-use regulation, housing price appreciation, tenure patterns, and household wealth by age cohort. A useful design would compare high-demand metros with differing supply constraints, exploiting variation in geographic limits, zoning restrictiveness, and permitting responsiveness. The analysis would estimate whether price growth in constrained metros disproportionately increases net worth among older homeowner cohorts while reducing wealth accumulation among younger renters and marginal first-time buyers.\n\nA key contribution would be to connect housing-market institutions to the intergenerational transmission of inequality. The project would examine whether parental housing wealth becomes more important for young households’ ability to buy in constrained metros, thereby turning local supply restrictions into a mechanism that reinforces family-background inequality. This could be tested by studying down-payment assistance, timing of first home purchase, and cohort-specific wealth trajectories across metropolitan areas.\n\nThe expected contribution is both theoretical and policy-relevant. If supply constraints raise aggregate homeowner wealth while excluding younger and less-wealthy cohorts from ownership, then land-use regulation affects not only affordability but the distribution of wealth across generations. The findings would inform debates over zoning reform, housing construction, property taxation, and policies aimed at reducing wealth inequality in high-productivity urban regions."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/clinical_medicine] Discuss the role of biomarkers and imaging in guiding immunotherapy decisions for advanced non-small cell lung cancer. Respond only as free_text.
full_prompt: Discuss the role of biomarkers and imaging in guiding immunotherapy decisions for advanced non-small cell lung cancer. Respond only as free_text.
completion:
Rationale: I first searched for high-impact clinical evidence anchoring immunotherapy selection in advanced NSCLC, emphasizing biomarkers and imaging endpoints.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"advanced non-small cell lung cancer immunotherapy PD-L1 tumor mutational burden imaging RECIST iRECIST pembrolizumab nivolumab atezolizumab","discipline":"clinical_medicine","limit":5}
```
[result]: Candidate evidence included KEYNOTE-024 (pembrolizumab in PD-L1 TPS ≥50%; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1606774), KEYNOTE-189 (pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy in nonsquamous NSCLC; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1801005), CheckMate 227 (nivolumab plus ipilimumab and tumor mutational burden/PD-L1 subgroups; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1910231), IMpower150 (atezolizumab/bevacizumab/chemotherapy; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1716948), and iRECIST guidance for immunotherapy response assessment (DOI 10.1016/S1470-2045(17)30074-8). OpenAlex W identifiers were not reliably available in this simulated retrieval, so DOI identifiers were retained.

Rationale: I read the core trial and response-assessment evidence to identify clinically actionable decision points.
```action
{"tool":"read","candidates":["doi:10.1056/NEJMoa1606774","doi:10.1056/NEJMoa1801005","doi:10.1056/NEJMoa1910231","doi:10.1016/S1470-2045(17)30074-8"],"focus":"biomarkers and imaging used to guide immunotherapy decisions in advanced NSCLC"}
```
[result]: PD-L1 tumor proportion score remains the most routinely used biomarker for first-line immune checkpoint inhibitor selection. High PD-L1 expression supports single-agent pembrolizumab or other PD-1/PD-L1 approaches in selected patients, while lower or negative PD-L1 more often supports chemoimmunotherapy. TMB has biologic and historical interest but limited routine first-line use because assay variability and inconsistent predictive value reduce clinical confidence. Imaging evidence supports CT-based RECIST for standard response assessment, with iRECIST used when pseudoprogression is suspected and the patient remains clinically stable.

Rationale: I extracted the practical biomarker framework, including markers that argue against immediate immunotherapy.
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","source":"advanced NSCLC biomarker guidelines and immunotherapy trials","entities":["PD-L1","EGFR","ALK","ROS1","BRAF","MET exon 14","RET","NTRK","ERBB2","KRAS G12C","STK11","KEAP1","TMB","MSI-H","ctDNA"]}
```
[result]: Actionable oncogenic drivers strongly shape immunotherapy sequencing. EGFR-mutant and ALK-positive NSCLC generally respond less well to single-agent checkpoint blockade and should usually receive targeted therapy first; immunotherapy given before some TKIs may increase toxicity risk. Broader molecular profiling, including ROS1, RET, MET exon 14, BRAF V600E, NTRK, ERBB2, and KRAS G12C, prevents premature use of immunotherapy when targeted options are preferable. STK11/KEAP1 co-alterations and liver metastases may indicate poorer immunotherapy outcomes, while MSI-H is rare but supportive of checkpoint blockade.

Rationale: I checked whether an integrated biomarker-imaging decision framework would add value beyond existing isolated discussions.
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"A clinically integrated framework combining PD-L1, broad genomic testing, ctDNA, TMB context, and serial CT/PET/MRI interpretation can better guide immunotherapy initiation, sequencing, and continuation decisions in advanced NSCLC.","discipline":"clinical_medicine"}
```
[result]: The concept is not wholly novel, but it remains clinically important because biomarkers and imaging are often discussed separately. A useful synthesis would emphasize how molecular results determine whether immunotherapy should be delayed, how PD-L1 and clinical features guide regimen intensity, and how imaging distinguishes true progression from immune-related response patterns or toxicity.

{"tool": "propose", "answer": "Biomarkers and imaging should be viewed as complementary tools rather than separate steps in choosing immunotherapy for advanced non-small cell lung cancer. The central biomarker remains PD-L1 immunohistochemistry: patients with high PD-L1 expression, especially tumor proportion score ≥50% and no targetable driver alteration, may be candidates for single-agent PD-1/PD-L1 blockade, whereas patients with PD-L1 1–49% or negative tumors more commonly receive chemoimmunotherapy or, in selected cases, dual-checkpoint approaches. However, PD-L1 is imperfect because expression is heterogeneous, assay-dependent, and influenced by sampling site and prior therapy.\n\nComprehensive molecular testing is equally important because it can redirect treatment away from immunotherapy. EGFR-mutant and ALK-positive tumors, and many other oncogene-driven cancers such as ROS1, RET, MET exon 14, BRAF V600E, NTRK, ERBB2, or KRAS G12C subsets, often have preferred targeted therapies. In these settings, giving immunotherapy first can reduce benefit and may complicate subsequent targeted therapy through increased toxicity. Tumor mutational burden, MSI-H status, STK11/KEAP1 alterations, smoking history, and circulating tumor DNA can add context, but most are not yet as decisive as PD-L1 plus broad genomic profiling in routine first-line decisions.\n\nImaging guides both eligibility and ongoing management. Baseline CT chest/abdomen, PET-CT when appropriate, and brain MRI help define disease burden, metastatic sites, and symptoms that may favor rapid cytoreduction with chemoimmunotherapy rather than slower single-agent immunotherapy. During treatment, serial CT using RECIST remains the standard for response evaluation, but immune-related patterns require caution: pseudoprogression is uncommon in NSCLC but possible, so iRECIST-style confirmation can be reasonable when radiographic progression occurs in a clinically stable patient. Conversely, rapid clinical decline with enlarging lesions should generally be treated as true progression or hyperprogression.\n\nImaging also identifies complications that directly affect immunotherapy decisions, including immune-related pneumonitis, sarcoid-like nodal reactions, adrenalitis, colitis, and brain progression. FDG-PET may help clarify metabolic response or equivocal lesions, but inflammation can mimic progression, so PET findings should be interpreted with clinical context and anatomic imaging. The best clinical approach is therefore integrated: use biomarkers to choose the initial strategy, use imaging to confirm stage, tempo, response, and toxicity, and reassess both molecular and radiographic information at progression to guide subsequent therapy or clinical trial enrollment."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/robotics_control] Analyze control strategies for compliant robotic manipulation in uncertain contact-rich tasks, such as assembly or household object handling. Emphasize impedanc
full_prompt: Analyze control strategies for compliant robotic manipulation in uncertain contact-rich tasks, such as assembly or household object handling. Emphasize impedance control, hybrid force-position control, and learning-based adaptation.
completion:
brief rationale: Establish the core literature spine for contact-rich manipulation control, covering classical impedance, hybrid force-position control, and recent adaptive/learning residual methods.
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "robot compliant manipulation uncertain contact-rich tasks impedance control hybrid force position control learning adaptation assembly household manipulation", "candidates": ["Hogan, 1985, Impedance Control: An Approach to Manipulation", "Raibert & Craig, 1981, Hybrid Position/Force Control of Manipulators", "Khatib, 1987, A Unified Approach for Motion and Force Control of Robot Manipulators", "Johannink et al., 2019, Residual Reinforcement Learning for Robot Control", "Lee et al., 2020, Learning quadrupedal/contact-rich control via adaptation where relevant to residual compliance ideas"]} 
```
[result]: Found a coherent research gap: classical impedance and hybrid force-position control provide stability and interpretability in contact, but require hand-designed stiffness, damping, contact frames, and switching logic. Learning-based approaches improve adaptation to uncertain geometry, friction, and object variation, but often lack passivity, force safety, and generalization guarantees. A promising direction is a structured controller in which learning adapts impedance parameters, contact modes, and residual force/motion policies while a passivity-aware low-level controller enforces safety.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "This project will develop and evaluate control strategies for compliant robotic manipulation in uncertain, contact-rich tasks such as peg insertion, drawer opening, cable routing, and household object handling. The central hypothesis is that robust manipulation requires combining the safety and interpretability of impedance and hybrid force-position control with learning-based adaptation that can infer task-specific contact conditions online.\n\nThe proposed framework will use impedance control as the default interaction layer, regulating apparent stiffness and damping to absorb pose uncertainty and reduce damaging contact forces. On top of this, a hybrid force-position module will explicitly separate constrained and unconstrained directions, allowing the robot to track motion where free space is available while regulating force along contact normals. Because real household and assembly settings rarely provide exact contact frames or friction parameters, these quantities will be estimated and updated during execution.\n\nLearning will be introduced in a structured rather than fully black-box manner. A policy or residual learner will adapt impedance gains, select hybrid control modes, and compensate for unmodeled contact dynamics, while a passivity or control-barrier safety layer limits commanded forces, energy injection, and unstable gain changes. This should preserve the reliability of model-based control while improving performance under geometry variation, compliance uncertainty, and unexpected collisions.\n\nEvaluation will compare fixed impedance control, hand-designed hybrid force-position control, pure learned policies, and the proposed adaptive compliant controller across controlled assembly benchmarks and everyday manipulation tasks. Metrics will include success rate, peak contact force, completion time, recovery from pose error, transfer to unseen objects, and stability under friction or stiffness changes. The expected outcome is a practical control architecture that clarifies when to rely on impedance, when to enforce hybrid force-position constraints, and how learning can safely adapt both in uncertain contact-rich manipulation."}
```

topic: [en/free_text_answer/robotics_control] Identify open research challenges in safe human-robot physical interaction control for collaborative manipulators. Your answer should use the free_text schema o
full_prompt: Identify open research challenges in safe human-robot physical interaction control for collaborative manipulators. Your answer should use the free_text schema only.
completion:
I’ll first scan for representative recent and foundational work to anchor the challenge framing.  
```action search
{"query":"safe physical human-robot interaction control collaborative manipulators impedance admittance safety survey open challenges","discipline":"robotics_control","limit":5}
```  
[result]: Found candidate themes: injury biomechanics and collision limits in pHRI; impedance/admittance and variable-stiffness control; energy/passivity-based safety; human-intention-aware shared control; learning-based control with safety filters. Representative sources include Haddadin et al., “Safe physical human-robot interaction: measurements, analysis and new insights,” IJRR 2009; De Santis et al., “An atlas of physical human–robot interaction,” Mechanism and Machine Theory 2008; and Villani et al., “Survey on human–robot collaboration in industrial settings,” Mechatronics 2018. OpenAlex IDs were not returned in the cached search result.

I’ll check whether a proposal focused on unresolved control challenges is distinct from standard survey coverage.  
```action novelty_check
{"claim":"Open research challenges remain in unifying model-based safety, adaptive physical interaction control, real-time human intent estimation, and certifiable learning for collaborative manipulators operating under intermittent contact and task uncertainty.","against":["pHRI safety surveys","industrial HRC surveys","impedance/admittance control surveys","safe learning control papers"]}
```  
[result]: Novelty assessment: incremental as a survey topic, but promising as a focused research agenda if framed around verification-ready controllers that combine contact-aware dynamics, online human-state inference, and standards-compatible safety guarantees under nonstationary human behavior.

{"tool":"propose","answer":"A useful research direction is to treat safe human-robot physical interaction control not as a single collision-avoidance problem, but as a layered control problem spanning prediction, contact regulation, energy management, and certification. Collaborative manipulators still struggle when interaction changes from planned guidance to unexpected impact, sustained contact, or co-manipulation with ambiguous human intent. Open challenges include defining safety metrics that go beyond speed-and-separation monitoring, especially metrics that capture pain, injury risk, startle, loss of balance, and task-dependent human vulnerability.\n\nA second challenge is robust interaction control under uncertainty. Impedance, admittance, and passivity-based methods are mature, but their tuning often assumes known payloads, predictable contacts, or conservative stiffness limits. Future controllers need to adapt apparent inertia, damping, and stiffness online while preserving formal safety and stability guarantees. This is particularly difficult for lightweight cobots that must remain productive while interacting with humans whose arm impedance, intent, and reaction delays vary across time and individuals.\n\nA third open problem is integrating learning into safety-critical pHRI control. Learning methods can estimate human intent, personalize assistance, and improve contact-rich manipulation, but certification remains weak when policies adapt online. Promising work should combine model predictive control, control barrier functions, passivity observers, and reachability analysis with learned human models, so that adaptation is constrained by verifiable safety envelopes rather than post-hoc testing.\n\nFinally, the field needs better benchmarks and validation protocols. Many pHRI controllers are evaluated on narrow laboratory tasks with healthy expert participants, while real deployments involve fatigue, distraction, occlusion, tool use, and multi-human workspaces. A strong research agenda would develop shared datasets, reproducible contact-rich tasks, and standards-aligned test procedures that connect controller performance to measurable human safety, comfort, and trust."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/energy] Assess the lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions of utility-scale solar PV compared with onshore wind and natural gas combined-cycle power plants. Respond only as
full_prompt: Assess the lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions of utility-scale solar PV compared with onshore wind and natural gas combined-cycle power plants. Respond only as free_text.
completion:
Brief rationale: I first locate authoritative lifecycle-assessment syntheses that report comparable greenhouse gas intensities for solar PV, onshore wind, and natural gas combined-cycle generation.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions utility-scale solar PV onshore wind natural gas combined cycle meta-analysis NREL IPCC"}
```

[result]: Simulated search returned high-relevance candidates: NREL Life Cycle Assessment Harmonization studies for PV, wind, and natural gas; IPCC AR6 lifecycle emissions ranges for electricity technologies; and peer-reviewed LCA synthesis papers on electricity generation. Candidate records included DOI/report identifiers; OpenAlex IDs were not available in the cached search result.

Brief rationale: I then extract the core comparative values and methodological issues: median gCO2e/kWh, lifecycle stages included, and drivers of variation.

```action
{"tool": "read", "candidates": ["NREL LCA Harmonization PV/Wind/Natural Gas synthesis", "IPCC AR6 WGIII electricity lifecycle emissions annex", "peer-reviewed electricity-generation LCA reviews"]}
```

[result]: Simulated reading indicates typical harmonized median lifecycle emissions of roughly 40–50 gCO2e/kWh for utility-scale solar PV, about 10–15 gCO2e/kWh for onshore wind, and about 450–500 gCO2e/kWh for natural gas combined-cycle plants without carbon capture. Main uncertainty drivers are PV module manufacturing energy mix and efficiency, wind capacity factor and materials, methane leakage for gas supply, plant heat rate, and assumed lifetime/capacity factor.

{"tool": "propose", "answer": "This assessment should compare utility-scale solar PV, onshore wind, and natural gas combined-cycle power plants on a harmonized lifecycle greenhouse-gas basis, expressed in grams of CO2-equivalent per kilowatt-hour delivered. The system boundary should include raw material extraction, component manufacturing, transport, construction, operation and maintenance, fuel supply where relevant, and end-of-life treatment. For natural gas combined-cycle plants, upstream methane leakage and combustion emissions must be included because they dominate total lifecycle impacts.\n\nBased on major lifecycle-assessment syntheses, onshore wind has the lowest typical lifecycle emissions, commonly around 10–15 gCO2e/kWh. Utility-scale solar PV is also low-carbon but generally higher than wind, often around 40–50 gCO2e/kWh, largely because of energy-intensive module and balance-of-system manufacturing. Natural gas combined-cycle generation is much higher, typically around 450–500 gCO2e/kWh without carbon capture, with most emissions from fuel combustion and additional contributions from gas extraction, processing, transport, and methane leakage.\n\nThe proposed research would update these comparisons using current technology assumptions: higher-efficiency PV modules, larger wind turbines, changing manufacturing electricity mixes, and recent methane-leakage estimates. A sensitivity analysis should vary capacity factor, plant lifetime, PV supply-chain location, gas heat rate, methane leakage rate, and global warming potential time horizon. This is important because the relative ranking is robust, but the magnitude of the gap—especially between solar PV and wind, and between gas and renewables—depends on these assumptions.\n\nThe expected conclusion is that both utility-scale solar PV and onshore wind offer lifecycle greenhouse-gas reductions of roughly an order of magnitude or more compared with unabated natural gas combined-cycle power. Wind is likely to remain the lowest-emitting option on a lifecycle basis, while solar PV remains strongly favorable relative to gas despite manufacturing-related emissions. The study should present median estimates, uncertainty ranges, and scenario-based sensitivities to support power-sector planning and decarbonization policy."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/robotics_control] Investigate how reinforcement learning policies can be safely combined with classical feedback controllers for high-speed quadrotor flight. Respond only as free
full_prompt: Investigate how reinforcement learning policies can be safely combined with classical feedback controllers for high-speed quadrotor flight. Respond only as free_text.
completion:
I first map the most relevant research neighborhoods: agile quadrotor flight, learning-based control, safety filters, and hybrid RL/classical feedback architectures.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "reinforcement learning safe classical feedback control high-speed quadrotor flight agile drones geometric controller MPC safety filter", "discipline": "robotics_control", "top_k": 6}
```

[result]: Candidates identified: (1) high-speed quadrotor flight with model-predictive or time-optimal control; (2) neural/RL policies for agile quadrotor maneuvers; (3) residual reinforcement learning layered on top of stabilizing controllers; (4) control barrier functions and safety filters for learning-enabled robots; (5) geometric SE(3) quadrotor control as a reliable classical feedback backbone. Representative papers include work by Foehn et al. on time-optimal quadrotor flight, Kaufmann et al. on learning agile quadrotor control, Lee/Leok/McClamroch on geometric tracking control, and recent safe-RL / CBF-MPC literature. OpenAlex IDs were not reliably available in the simulated index for all candidates.

I then read the core threads to identify what each contributes and where the integration gaps remain.

```action
{"tool": "read", "candidates": ["Foehn et al. time-optimal planning/control for quadrotors", "Kaufmann et al. learning agile quadrotor flight", "Lee et al. geometric tracking control of quadrotor UAVs on SE(3)", "residual reinforcement learning for robot control", "CBF safety filters for learning-based control"]}
```

[result]: Classical controllers provide strong local stability, interpretability, and predictable failure modes, but can be conservative or brittle under aerodynamic effects at high speed. RL policies can exploit unmodeled dynamics and improve aggressive maneuvering, but often lack hard safety guarantees under distribution shift. Residual RL is promising because it keeps a trusted nominal controller in the loop, yet existing work often evaluates safety empirically rather than certifying the combined closed-loop system. CBF or MPC safety filters can enforce constraints, but at high-speed quadrotor regimes they must handle actuator saturation, state-estimation delay, drag, and large attitude excursions.

Next I extract a compact “research genome” from the literature: assumptions, mechanisms, safety guarantees, and missing experiments.

```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "papers": ["geometric quadrotor control", "agile RL quadrotor flight", "residual RL", "CBF safety filters", "high-speed MPC quadrotor flight"], "features": ["controller role", "learning role", "safety mechanism", "flight regime", "validation gap"]}
```

[result]: Genome extracted: nominal SE(3) or MPC controllers are typically used for tracking and stabilization; learned policies are used either as full end-to-end controllers, residual force/torque corrections, or trajectory-selection modules; safety is handled by constraint projection, backup controllers, domain randomization, or post-hoc testing; most methods validate on moderate-speed tasks or isolated agile maneuvers rather than sustained high-speed flight through constrained environments; few works jointly evaluate formal safety, aerodynamic adaptation, and real-time feasibility on onboard compute.

I compare this genome against a candidate contribution to ensure the proposal is not just “RL plus controller” but a testable new hybrid-control architecture.

```action
{"tool": "genome_diff", "candidate_idea": "A certifiable residual-RL architecture in which a learned policy proposes bounded corrections to a geometric/MPC quadrotor controller, while a real-time safety filter enforces control authority limits, CBF constraints, and a recoverability condition to a backup stabilizing controller during high-speed flight.", "baseline_genome": "existing agile quadrotor RL, residual RL, CBF safety filters, geometric/MPC control"}
```

[result]: Differentiators: combines residual RL with explicit authority allocation rather than unrestricted policy output; makes the safety filter aware of high-speed aerodynamic envelopes and actuator saturation; includes a recoverability certificate to a classical backup controller, not only instantaneous obstacle avoidance; proposes experiments that sweep speed, perception latency, wind, and domain shift. Main risks: CBF feasibility at aggressive attitudes, conservatism of safety filter, sim-to-real transfer of residual dynamics model.

Finally I check novelty and refine the proposal around concrete hypotheses, metrics, and validation.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "claim": "Safe combination of RL residual policies with classical feedback controllers for high-speed quadrotor flight using control-authority bounds, CBF/MPC safety filtering, and backup recoverability certificates.", "discipline": "robotics_control"}
```

[result]: Novelty assessment: moderately high if framed as a unified high-speed flight architecture with certified residual authority and empirical real-robot validation. The individual components exist separately, but the combination of residual learning, aggressive-flight aerodynamic compensation, hard safety filtering, and recoverability-to-backup-controller evaluation appears underexplored. Recommended proposal: emphasize closed-loop safety under learning-induced residuals, characterize when the filter overrides RL, and benchmark against pure geometric control, MPC, unconstrained RL, and residual RL without certification.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "This project will investigate a hybrid control architecture for high-speed quadrotor flight in which reinforcement learning is used only as a bounded residual policy around a trusted classical feedback controller. The nominal controller will be a geometric SE(3) tracker or nonlinear MPC designed to stabilize aggressive trajectories, while the learned policy will compensate for unmodeled high-speed effects such as drag, propeller interactions, actuator delay, and trajectory-dependent aerodynamic disturbances. The central hypothesis is that RL can improve tracking and agility without sacrificing safety if its authority is explicitly limited and mediated by a real-time safety layer.\n\nThe proposed method will combine three safety mechanisms. First, the residual policy will output corrections in a constrained control subspace, with magnitude limits tied to the stability margin of the nominal controller. Second, a control barrier function or fast MPC safety filter will project the combined command onto a safe set encoding obstacle clearance, actuator saturation, attitude limits, and velocity-dependent flight-envelope constraints. Third, the system will maintain a recoverability certificate: if the learned residual or current state threatens feasibility, control authority reverts to a backup stabilizing controller with a verified region of attraction.\n\nTraining will proceed in simulation with domain randomization over mass, inertia, thrust curves, drag parameters, latency, wind, and sensor noise. The reward will encourage high-speed tracking performance and energy efficiency, but constraint satisfaction will be handled by the safety layer rather than learned solely from penalties. This separation allows the research to study when learning improves the nominal controller, when the safety filter suppresses the policy, and how much performance is lost to conservatism.\n\nEvaluation will compare pure geometric control, nonlinear MPC, unconstrained RL, standard residual RL, and the proposed certified residual-RL controller on aggressive waypoint flight, gate traversal, and disturbance-rejection tasks. Metrics will include lap time or traversal speed, tracking error, minimum obstacle distance, actuator saturation frequency, safety-filter intervention rate, recovery success rate, and sim-to-real degradation. The expected contribution is a practical framework for safely exploiting RL in high-speed quadrotor regimes while preserving the interpretability and fallback guarantees of classical feedback control."}
```

Fewer papers jointly compare energy and agricultural commodities under one identification framework from 2020 onward. A strong contribution would separate broad geopolitical news from commodity-specific transmission channels; compare global benchmark prices with regionally segmented prices; test nonlinear effects during stress periods; and evaluate whether GPR shocks transmit through volatility, inventories, exchange rates, sanctions, and futures-market expectations.

Now I formulate the research proposal around an integrated empirical design for the three commodity markets.
```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"<proposal>"}
```
[result]: {"tool":"propose","answer":"This project will assess how geopolitical risk has affected global commodity prices since 2020, with emphasis on crude oil, natural gas, and wheat. The central research question is whether geopolitical risk shocks have had common effects across these markets or whether the transmission mechanisms differ by commodity because of storage, transportability, regional market segmentation, and exposure to the Russia–Ukraine war. The study will focus on the COVID-19 period, the 2021–2022 energy crisis, the February 2022 invasion of Ukraine, sanctions episodes, Black Sea export disruptions, and subsequent energy and food-market adjustments.\n\nEmpirically, the project will combine daily and weekly commodity price data for Brent and WTI crude oil, European TTF and U.S. Henry Hub natural gas, and CBOT wheat futures with the Caldara-Iacoviello geopolitical risk index, Russia-specific geopolitical indicators, sanctions-event dummies, inventory measures, exchange rates, global demand proxies, and freight or shipping indicators. The baseline approach will use local projections and structural VAR models to estimate the dynamic response of prices and volatility to geopolitical risk shocks. To capture crisis-period asymmetry, the study will also estimate quantile regressions or regime-switching models that test whether GPR effects are stronger during high-price or high-volatility states.\n\nThe expected hypothesis is that oil prices respond positively to geopolitical risk through global supply-risk premia, but the magnitude varies with spare capacity and inventory conditions. Natural gas should show the strongest regional heterogeneity: European TTF prices are expected to be far more sensitive to geopolitical risk than Henry Hub because of Europe’s pre-2022 reliance on Russian pipeline gas, limited short-run substitution, and storage constraints. Wheat prices are expected to react sharply to Russia–Ukraine-specific conflict and Black Sea logistics shocks, with additional amplification through fertilizer and energy costs.\n\nThe contribution is to provide a unified post-2020 comparison of energy and agricultural commodity markets rather than treating oil, gas, and wheat separately. By distinguishing broad geopolitical news from commodity-specific conflict channels, the project can clarify whether geopolitical risk is best understood as a general macro-financial uncertainty shock or as a set of market-specific supply-chain disruptions. The findings would be relevant for inflation forecasting, commodity hedging, energy-security policy, and food-security risk management."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/robotics_control] Develop a research proposal for adaptive control of soft robots with highly nonlinear dynamics and limited sensing. Respond only as free_text and include key hy
full_prompt: Develop a research proposal for adaptive control of soft robots with highly nonlinear dynamics and limited sensing. Respond only as free_text and include key hypotheses, methods, and evaluation metrics.
completion:
I’ll first ground the proposal in canonical soft-robot control and learning literature, prioritizing work on continuum dynamics, model-based control, and sensing limitations.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "soft robot adaptive control nonlinear dynamics limited sensing model predictive control learning observers OpenAlex"}
```

[result]: Found candidate themes: model-based control for soft continuum robots, Koopman/operator-learning approaches for nonlinear dynamics, adaptive/robust control under unmodeled deformation, and proprioceptive state estimation with sparse sensors. Candidate papers include model predictive control for soft robots, learning-based control of continuum manipulators, and reduced-order modeling for soft robotic dynamics; OpenAlex IDs were not reliably available in the local simulated index, so candidates were retained by title/venue/DOI where possible.

Next, I’ll read across the most relevant bodies of work to identify the unresolved gap: high nonlinearity plus partial observability in real-time adaptive control.

```action
{"tool": "read", "candidates": ["Model predictive control for soft robots", "Learning-based control of soft continuum manipulators", "Reduced-order modeling and control of soft robots", "Koopman operator methods for robotic control", "State estimation for soft robots with embedded and sparse sensing"]}
```

[result]: The literature suggests that accurate soft-robot control is limited by hysteresis, contact-dependent deformation, actuator variability, and expensive state measurement. Purely model-free policies can adapt but are data-hungry and brittle outside training conditions. Purely analytical models are interpretable but often too inaccurate for complex geometries and materials. A promising gap is adaptive control that combines low-dimensional learned dynamics, online uncertainty estimation, and active sensing/state estimation.

I’ll extract the “research genome”: core assumptions, mechanisms, hypotheses, and measurable variables.

```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "papers": ["soft_robot_mpc", "koopman_soft_robot_control", "adaptive_continuum_control", "sparse_soft_robot_state_estimation"]}
```

[result]: Extracted elements:  
- State representation: low-dimensional latent coordinates inferred from limited proprioceptive/exteroceptive sensing.  
- Dynamics: highly nonlinear, history-dependent, actuator- and environment-dependent.  
- Control strategy: adaptive MPC or robust adaptive policy using learned residual dynamics.  
- Sensing: sparse strain, pressure, IMU, tendon/pressure inputs, occasional vision or endpoint tracking.  
- Evaluation variables: trajectory error, disturbance rejection, adaptation speed, sample efficiency, computational latency, energy use, and safety constraint violations.

I’ll compare this genome to the target topic and identify the novelty wedge.

```action
{"tool": "genome_diff", "target": "adaptive control of soft robots with highly nonlinear dynamics and limited sensing", "baseline_genome": "existing soft-robot MPC, model-free RL, Koopman control, and sparse state estimation"}
```

[result]: Novelty gap: existing methods usually optimize either dynamics learning, control, or state estimation separately. The proposed contribution should integrate all three: an adaptive latent-state observer, uncertainty-aware residual dynamics learning, and constrained real-time control. High-value novelty lies in treating limited sensing not as a passive constraint but as part of the control loop via active information-seeking motions and online sensor reliability weighting.

Before proposing, I’ll run a novelty check against likely neighboring ideas.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "idea": "uncertainty-aware adaptive latent-state MPC for soft robots with sparse sensing and active information gathering"}
```

[result]: The idea is related to existing learning MPC and latent dynamics work, but appears distinguishable if framed around: 1) joint online adaptation of observer and dynamics model, 2) explicit sensing-limited control with uncertainty-aware objectives, 3) real-time deployment on physical soft robots under actuator drift, payload changes, and partial sensor failure, and 4) evaluation against both model-free and fixed-model baselines.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "This project proposes an adaptive control framework for soft robots operating under highly nonlinear, hysteretic dynamics and limited sensing. The central hypothesis is that reliable real-time control can be achieved by jointly learning a compact latent state, adapting residual dynamics online, and explicitly accounting for sensing uncertainty inside the controller. A second hypothesis is that active information-seeking control actions, when safely embedded in task execution, will improve robustness to actuator drift, payload changes, contact, and partial sensor degradation without requiring dense motion-capture feedback.\n\nThe method will combine three modules. First, a sparse-sensing observer will infer a low-dimensional deformation state from limited measurements such as pressure, tendon displacement, strain gauges, IMUs, or intermittent vision. Second, a physics-informed latent dynamics model will represent nominal robot behavior, while an online residual learner will adapt to unmodeled effects including material fatigue, hysteresis, and environmental contact. Third, an uncertainty-aware adaptive model predictive controller will choose actions that trade off task tracking, safety constraints, energy use, and information gain. The controller will update its belief over state and dynamics in real time, allowing it to compensate for nonlinear behavior without relying on full-state sensing.\n\nExperiments will use one or more benchmark soft robotic platforms, such as a pneumatically actuated continuum arm, a soft gripper, or a tendon-driven soft manipulator. The system will be tested on trajectory tracking, reaching, object interaction, payload variation, and disturbance-rejection tasks. Baselines will include fixed-model MPC, model-free reinforcement learning, nonadaptive learned dynamics control, and classical feedback controllers using the same sensor suite. Ablations will isolate the effect of latent-state estimation, residual adaptation, uncertainty-aware control, and active sensing.\n\nEvaluation metrics will include endpoint/root-mean-square trajectory error, task success rate, adaptation time after a dynamics shift, robustness to sensor dropout, disturbance recovery time, safety constraint violations, control effort, energy consumption, computational latency, and sample efficiency. Success would be demonstrated by lower tracking error and faster recovery than nonadaptive methods under limited sensing, while maintaining real-time control rates and reducing dependence on external motion-capture systems. The expected contribution is a general adaptive control architecture that makes soft robots more deployable in practical settings where dynamics are uncertain, sensors are sparse, and operating conditions change over time."}
```

topic: [en/free_text_answer/economics_finance] Research the role of algorithmic trading in modern equity markets, focusing on liquidity, volatility, and flash-crash risk. Use the free-text answer schema only
full_prompt: Research the role of algorithmic trading in modern equity markets, focusing on liquidity, volatility, and flash-crash risk. Use the free-text answer schema only.
completion:
I first scope the empirical literature around algorithmic trading (AT), high-frequency trading (HFT), liquidity, volatility, and crash dynamics, prioritizing peer-reviewed market microstructure papers and regulator-adjacent evidence.
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "algorithmic trading liquidity volatility flash crash equity markets empirical evidence Hendershott Jones Menkveld Brogaard Kirilenko", "discipline": "economics_finance"}
```
[result]: Found core candidates: Hendershott, Jones & Menkveld, “Does Algorithmic Trading Improve Liquidity?” Journal of Finance, DOI: 10.1111/j.1540-6261.2011.01624.x; Brogaard, Hendershott & Riordan, “High-Frequency Trading and Price Discovery,” Review of Financial Studies, DOI: 10.1093/rfs/hht045; Kirilenko, Kyle, Samadi & Tuzun, “The Flash Crash: High-Frequency Trading in an Electronic Market,” Journal of Finance, DOI: 10.1111/jofi.12498; Hasbrouck & Saar, “Low-Latency Trading,” Journal of Financial Markets, DOI: 10.1016/j.finmar.2013.05.003. OpenAlex IDs were not returned by the simulated search index for these candidates.

I then read the central liquidity paper to establish the baseline finding: whether automation improves quoted and effective liquidity under normal market conditions.
```action
{"tool": "read", "candidates": [{"title": "Does Algorithmic Trading Improve Liquidity?", "authors": "Hendershott, Jones, Menkveld", "venue": "Journal of Finance", "doi": "10.1111/j.1540-6261.2011.01624.x"}]}
```
[result]: Key finding: algorithmic trading is associated with narrower spreads, lower adverse selection, and more informative quotes, especially for large-cap stocks. The paper uses the NYSE autoquote introduction as an instrument-like event increasing algorithmic activity. Interpretation: AT generally improves liquidity in continuous markets, but the benefits are conditional on stable infrastructure and incentives.

Next I read evidence on price discovery and volatility to distinguish benign liquidity provision from potentially destabilizing speed-based trading.
```action
{"tool": "read", "candidates": [{"title": "High-Frequency Trading and Price Discovery", "authors": "Brogaard, Hendershott, Riordan", "venue": "Review of Financial Studies", "doi": "10.1093/rfs/hht045"}, {"title": "Low-Latency Trading", "authors": "Hasbrouck, Saar", "venue": "Journal of Financial Markets", "doi": "10.1016/j.finmar.2013.05.003"}]}
```
[result]: HFT activity contributes to price discovery and tends to trade in the direction of permanent price changes rather than transitory noise. Low-latency activity is associated with improved traditional liquidity measures and lower short-term volatility in many settings. However, the evidence also suggests that liquidity is endogenous and can disappear quickly when adverse selection risk rises.

I then focus on flash-crash risk, where the question shifts from average market quality to tail fragility and feedback under stress.
```action
{"tool": "read", "candidates": [{"title": "The Flash Crash: High-Frequency Trading in an Electronic Market", "authors": "Kirilenko, Kyle, Samadi, Tuzun", "venue": "Journal of Finance", "doi": "10.1111/jofi.12498"}, {"title": "The Microstructure of the Flash Crash", "authors": "Easley, López de Prado, O'Hara", "venue": "Journal of Portfolio Management", "doi": "10.3905/jpm.2011.37.2.118"}]}
```
[result]: Flash-crash evidence emphasizes interaction effects: large automated sell programs, inventory-constrained intermediaries, rapid order cancellations, and cross-market feedback can jointly produce extreme short-lived price moves. HFTs did not simply “cause” the Flash Crash, but their rapid liquidity taking and inventory management amplified order-flow imbalances once stress began.

I extract the conceptual genome of the literature: mechanisms, identification strategies, and unresolved tensions.
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "papers": ["Hendershott-Jones-Menkveld-2011", "Brogaard-Hendershott-Riordan-2014", "Hasbrouck-Saar-2013", "Kirilenko-Kyle-Samadi-Tuzun-2017", "Easley-LopezdePrado-OHara-2011"]}
```
[result]: Genome extracted: mechanisms include spread compression, faster quote updating, reduced stale quotes, adverse-selection screening, fleeting liquidity, endogenous liquidity withdrawal, latency races, inventory-risk limits, and cross-venue feedback. Common outcomes: quoted spread, effective spread, realized spread, price impact, depth, cancellation intensity, volatility, return autocorrelation, and extreme intraday drawdowns. Identification approaches include market-structure shocks, exchange-message data, proprietary HFT flags, regulatory datasets, and event studies around volatility interruptions.

I compare this genome against the research prompt to identify where a new project can add value.
```action
{"tool": "genome_diff", "target_topic": "role of algorithmic trading in modern equity markets, focusing on liquidity, volatility, and flash-crash risk", "existing_genome": "AT improves average liquidity but may increase fragility under stress; evidence is fragmented across normal-times market quality and rare tail events."}
```
[result]: Gap identified: the literature often separates normal-times liquidity benefits from stress-times liquidity fragility. A useful contribution would jointly estimate how algorithmic trading affects the entire distribution of liquidity and volatility, not only average spreads or isolated crash episodes. Strong design should distinguish liquidity provision from liquidity resilience: the ability of depth and spreads to recover after shocks.

I check whether the proposed angle is sufficiently novel and feasible within economics and finance.
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "proposal_seed": "Estimate whether algorithmic trading improves average liquidity while increasing left-tail liquidity fragility and flash-crash susceptibility across modern equity markets using intraday panel data, stress episodes, and market-structure shocks."}
```
[result]: Novelty assessment: moderately high. The average-liquidity channel is well studied, but a unified “liquidity resilience” framework linking algorithmic intensity, volatility regimes, order cancellation behavior, and crash-tail outcomes remains promising. Feasibility depends on access to high-frequency order-book or TAQ data and a credible proxy for algorithmic trading intensity, such as message traffic, cancellation-to-trade ratios, low-latency activity measures, or exchange technology shocks.

{"tool": "propose", "answer": "This research will examine the dual role of algorithmic trading in modern equity markets: its contribution to ordinary-time liquidity and price efficiency, and its potential to increase fragility during periods of stress. The central hypothesis is that algorithmic trading improves average market quality by narrowing spreads, accelerating price discovery, and reducing stale quotes, but that some of these benefits are state-dependent. In stressed conditions, the same speed, automation, and inventory controls that improve liquidity in normal times may lead to rapid cancellations, shallow displayed depth, and feedback dynamics that heighten flash-crash risk.\n\nThe empirical design should combine high-frequency equity market data with measures of algorithmic intensity, such as message traffic, cancellation-to-trade ratios, quote update frequency, or exchange-level technology shocks. The study would estimate effects on quoted spreads, effective spreads, market depth, realized volatility, price impact, and recovery time after liquidity shocks. Rather than focusing only on mean effects, the project should model the distribution of outcomes, especially the lower tail of liquidity and the upper tail of intraday volatility. This allows the research to distinguish liquidity provision from liquidity resilience.\n\nA credible identification strategy could use market-structure events that plausibly alter algorithmic participation, such as exchange automation upgrades, tick-size changes, co-location introductions, or regulatory interventions. The project could also compare normal periods with stress windows around macro announcements, market-wide selloffs, volatility halts, and extreme intraday dislocations. This would clarify whether algorithmic trading is stabilizing, destabilizing, or conditional on volatility regimes and inventory constraints.\n\nThe expected contribution is a unified framework for understanding algorithmic trading as both a source of efficiency and a possible source of tail risk. The research would inform debates over market design, including speed bumps, circuit breakers, minimum resting times, order-to-trade ratio rules, and volatility interruption mechanisms. Its policy relevance lies in identifying whether regulation should target algorithmic trading broadly, or instead focus on specific behaviors—such as excessive cancellation, fragile liquidity provision, and cross-venue feedback—that matter most during flash-crash episodes."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/energy] Analyze how grid-scale battery storage affects electricity market prices, renewable curtailment, and grid reliability in regions with high solar penetration. Us
full_prompt: Analyze how grid-scale battery storage affects electricity market prices, renewable curtailment, and grid reliability in regions with high solar penetration. Use the required free-text answer schema.
completion:

```action

```

[result]: Candidate literature includes empirical price-impact studies of utility-scale storage in CAISO/ERCOT, renewable-curtailment analyses for high-solar systems, and production-cost/unit-commitment studies on storage adequacy. OpenAlex-indexed candidates were found where available, including works associated with Paul Denholm, Robert Margolis, Mark Z. Jacobson, and Jesse D. Jenkins on storage, curtailment, and high-renewable grid operation; several newer market-design papers require DOI-to-OpenAlex resolution before citation locking.

Next I’ll extract the “research genome”: variables, methods, and unresolved causal questions.

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","sources":["CAISO battery storage price formation studies","ERCOT battery arbitrage and ancillary services studies","high-solar curtailment and reliability modeling papers"],"focus":["market prices","renewable curtailment","grid reliability","solar penetration","battery dispatch"]}
```

[result]: Common methods are production-cost modeling, dispatch simulation, reduced-form price regression, and capacity-expansion modeling. Core variables are battery power/energy capacity, solar share, net-load ramps, curtailment MWh, price volatility, negative-price frequency, LOLP/LOLE, reserve shortages, and ancillary-service prices. Main gap: many studies estimate storage value or curtailment reduction, but fewer jointly identify how batteries change energy prices, curtailment, and reliability under observed high-solar market operations while separating arbitrage, capacity, and reserve-service effects.

I’ll test whether the proposed angle is novel enough for a short research contribution.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"A comparative empirical-simulation study that quantifies how grid-scale batteries affect wholesale prices, solar curtailment, and reliability in high-solar regions by combining observed CAISO/ERCOT dispatch data with counterfactual production-cost simulations."}
```

[result]: Novelty is moderate-to-high if framed as a joint causal-counterfactual assessment rather than a storage valuation exercise. Strongest contribution: comparing regions with different market designs and solar profiles, estimating distributional price effects by hour/net-load condition, and linking curtailment reduction to reliability metrics rather than treating them separately.

{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project will analyze how grid-scale battery storage changes electricity market prices, renewable curtailment, and grid reliability in high-solar power systems. The study will focus on regions such as CAISO and ERCOT, where solar penetration is already high enough to create midday price suppression, negative-price events, steep evening ramps, and recurring renewable curtailment. The central research question is: under what operating conditions do batteries lower system costs and curtailment without creating new price volatility or reliability risks?\n\nThe empirical component will use market and operations data on hourly or sub-hourly prices, solar generation, battery charging and discharging, curtailment, load, reserve scarcity, and outage or emergency indicators. It will estimate how battery deployment affects price levels, price volatility, negative-price frequency, evening peak prices, and ancillary-service prices. The analysis will distinguish between batteries acting as energy arbitrage resources, reserve providers, and local congestion-management assets.\n\nThe modeling component will construct counterfactual dispatch simulations for selected high-solar periods, comparing observed system operation with scenarios that remove existing batteries, add incremental battery capacity, or vary battery duration. These simulations will estimate avoided solar curtailment, changes in net-load ramps, reserve shortfalls, and reliability metrics such as loss-of-load risk or scarcity-event frequency. Comparing CAISO and ERCOT will help identify how market design, reserve rules, interconnection constraints, and solar concentration affect the value and consequences of storage.\n\nThe expected contribution is a unified assessment of storage impacts across three outcomes that are often studied separately: market prices, renewable integration, and reliability. The project should clarify when batteries mainly shift cheap solar energy into evening peak hours, when they reduce curtailment, and when they provide measurable reliability benefits. The findings would inform storage procurement, market-design reforms, and planning rules for grids moving toward very high solar penetration."}

topic: [en/free_text_answer/interdisciplinary] Investigate how urban heat island mitigation strategies can integrate climate science, public health, and environmental justice to reduce heat-related mortality
full_prompt: Investigate how urban heat island mitigation strategies can integrate climate science, public health, and environmental justice to reduce heat-related mortality in low-income neighborhoods. Respond only as free_text.
completion:
I will first identify anchor literature spanning heat-health risk, urban heat island mitigation, and inequity to ground the proposal.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "urban heat island mitigation heat mortality environmental justice low income neighborhoods public health climate science"}
```

[result]: Key candidates found: Harlan et al., “Neighborhood microclimates and vulnerability to heat stress,” Social Science & Medicine, DOI: 10.1016/j.socscimed.2005.11.065; Reid et al., “Mapping community determinants of heat vulnerability,” Environmental Health Perspectives, DOI: 10.1289/ehp.0900683; Santamouris, “Cooling the cities—A review of reflective and green roof mitigation technologies,” Solar Energy, DOI: 10.1016/j.solener.2014.04.027; Shandas et al., “Integrating satellite and ground measurements for urban heat assessment,” Climate, DOI: 10.3390/cli7030041.

I will check whether the proposed integration is sufficiently distinct from existing heat-action planning work by emphasizing causal targeting, co-produced interventions, and mortality-reduction evaluation in low-income neighborhoods.

Existing work maps heat vulnerability or evaluates individual interventions such as trees, cool roofs, or heat warning systems, but fewer studies integrate fine-scale climate exposure modeling, health outcome prediction, distributive justice metrics, and participatory implementation into a single evaluation framework centered on mortality reduction.

{"tool": "propose", "answer": "This project will investigate how urban heat island mitigation can be designed to reduce heat-related mortality in low-income neighborhoods by integrating climate science, public health, and environmental justice. The central research question is: which combinations of built-environment cooling strategies—such as tree canopy expansion, cool roofs, reflective pavements, shade structures, and cooling-center access—produce the greatest health benefit for communities facing both high heat exposure and structural disadvantage?\n\nThe study will combine neighborhood-scale climate modeling with public health data on heat-related emergency visits, mortality, age, preexisting illness, housing quality, and air-conditioning access. Remote sensing and mobile temperature measurements will be used to identify intra-urban heat hot spots, while epidemiological models will estimate how changes in temperature exposure translate into avoided illness and deaths. Environmental justice analysis will assess whether current mitigation investments align with need or reproduce inequities by favoring wealthier, greener areas.\n\nA key contribution will be a co-produced prioritization framework developed with residents, local health departments, planners, and community organizations. Rather than treating low-income neighborhoods only as sites of vulnerability, the project will incorporate local knowledge about unsafe housing, work schedules, transit dependence, fear of public cooling centers, utility costs, and barriers to using green space. This will help distinguish interventions that are technically effective from those that are socially accessible and trusted.\n\nThe expected outcome is a decision-support model that ranks heat mitigation portfolios according to projected mortality reduction, cost, implementation feasibility, and equity impact. By linking physical cooling strategies to measurable public health outcomes and community-defined priorities, the research can guide cities toward heat adaptation policies that save lives while addressing the unequal distribution of climate risk."}

full_prompt: 请围绕金属有机框架材料（MOFs）在二氧化碳捕集中的结构调控策略，提出一个可验证的科研问题，并概述关键实验设计。请仅以 gene_genome 格式回答。
completion:
1. 先检索 MOFs 二氧化碳捕集的结构调控研究，以确定常见机制与可切入的结构变量。

[result]: 检索结果显示，开放金属位点、胺基/极性官能团修饰、孔径限域和疏水性调控是 MOFs 提升 CO2 吸附容量与选择性的主要策略；但多湿度条件下的结构—性能关联仍不充分。

